ref: The Confession of Peter · Matthew 16:13–16
text: Lord Jesus Christ,
whom blessed Peter confessed to be the true Son of God,
You too do I, unworthy, confess with my whole heart and mouth,
that You are the Christ, the Son of the living and eternal God;
and I beg You, most merciful Lord,
grant me to confess You in deed,
and by my works, denying You not, to be found faithful,
that I may obtain Your blessing with Peter,
and stand firm in faith and in work together with him.
Grant me also, my King and my God,
that, following You in conformity of will, I may fulfil Your good pleasure in all things,
and wholly abstain from all offense against You;
and so, by Your bounty, persevere unto the end. Amen.

ref: In the Beginning Was the Word · John 1:1–3
text: Lord Jesus, eternal Word of the Father,
you were in the beginning, the Principle from the Principle.
With thee is the Principle in the day of thy strength, in the splendours of the Saints;
from the womb, before Lucifer, you were begotten.
Your generation, who shall tell?
Be the beginning of my every intention,
and the end toward which I move,
that my acts may be divine,
since the beginning and the end are one and the same — you, my God.

ref: The Remedy Devised · none (Vita Christi, Part I, ch. II — pre-Incarnation; no Gospel quoted)
text: I beseech You, O Lord, let the word You have spoken be fulfilled.
Why, O Lord, have You made me?
For Truth herself knows that I perish, if You never show mercy.
The rational creature has need of Your pity,
for she is made wretched, and exceedingly miserable.
The time has come, O Lord, to have mercy upon her.

ref: The Flowering Rod · Isaiah 11:1 (with Numbers 17:8)
text: Lord Jesus, flower out of the root of Jesse,
You did not violate the Virgin, but adorned her the more with gifts and graces.
You are the flower in the promise, the fruit in grace and truth;
the nut that clung to the wood of the passion.
As the flower withers and the fruit is seen,
so let the carnal observance fall away in me,
and let grace and truth, made by You, be held fast.
In Your love let fear give its back and flee.

ref: The Forerunner Promised · Luke 1:5–13
text: Fear not, Zacharias, for your prayer is heard.
Lord, You did not despise the prayer of Your servant,
who prayed not for offspring he had ceased to hope for,
but for the sins of the people, and their redemption,
and the coming of the Messiah.
Make me also mindful of You,
to carry the memory of You in my breast,
for my own salvation and the salvation of others.
Let me enter Your temple by diligent care for Your worship,
and offer the incense of devout prayer.
And as You sent the Forerunner to prepare Your people,
preaching penance and faith,
so prepare my heart for the Saviour,
through whom grace and truth came to be.
Amen.

ref: The Word Made Flesh in Her · Luke 1:26–34
text: O Jesus, Son of the living God,
who by the will of the Father, the Holy Spirit working with Him,
came forth from that same Father's bosom
like a river flowing from a place of delight,
bending down through the lowly valleys,
looking upon the lowliness of Thy handmaid,
and didst descend into the womb of the Virgin,
where, conceived ineffably, Thou didst take flesh:
let Thy grace descend, O merciful Jesus, eagerly,
through the merits of the Mother and Virgin,
upon me, Thy most unworthy servant,
that through her I may desire Thee,
and within, in love, may conceive Thee;
and through that same grace working in me,
may bring forth the saving fruits of good works. Amen.

ref: The Leaping of the Forerunner · Luke 1:39-44, 57-63
text: Holy John the Baptist,
you who were filled with God before you were born from the womb,
knowing God before you were known in the world:
to you I flee, anxious, whom grace made so dear a friend of God —
I, whom my own iniquity made so guilty before God.
Remember, then, lord, that as the grace of God so raised you up,
so your mercy may lift up the one whom his own fault has so cast down.
My iniquity made me what I am; but you, not you, but the grace of God made you.
Obtain for me, a wretch, that as you exulted with great joy at the first coming of our Saviour,
so I too may be counted worthy to rejoice at His second coming,
and to exult with the saints in glory. Amen.

ref: The Book of His Generations · Matthew 1:1–2; Luke 3
text: Lord Jesus Christ, our hope and our trust,
be mindful of what You took upon Yourself for our redemption.
Be mindful that, the Creator of all things,
You deigned to take our nature and become aware of our frailty.
You came, O Lord, for the sake of sinners,
that You might blot out the sins of all.
What shall I render to You? What can be worthily done for this?
I praise You; I give You thanks with my whole heart,
for Your measureless benefits,
by which You came to the aid of a lost human race.
And I pray You, most merciful Lord,
that, having so bent down for us as to deign to become man,
You would not suffer to perish in us
what in mercy You resolved to assume.
Make me render to You a service grateful and owed. Amen.

ref: The Silence of Joseph · Matthew 1:18–21
text: Lord Jesus Christ, who art the impregnable wall of all who hope in thee,
be thou my refuge in tribulation;
behold my tribulations and my distresses, and have mercy on me,
and by all thy compassions come to my aid.
Behold my weakness, and with a father's mercy protect me,
that, upheld by thy providence, I may never be forsaken of thy consolation and mercy.
Remember, Lord, thy creature, and drive far from me the enemies that lie in wait for me;
that, fortified by thy mercy, I may know the sweetness of thy goodness in me,
and pay a worthy penance for my sins. Amen.

ref: The King Enrolled Among Us · Luke 2:1–5
text: Would that You, Lord Jesus,
would make me, a wretched one, to be among Your chosen,
that You might enroll me in eternity.
To You, our King, let me profess the tribute of faith and of justice,
in heart, in mouth, and in deed.

ref: The Knife of Stone · Luke 2:21
text: O merciful Jesus, who, born of the Virgin under the Law, willed to be circumcised:
circumcise, O merciful Jesus, the thoughts, the words, and the works of Your servant,
that I may carry through nothing contrary to Your will in thinking, in speaking, in working.
Behold, O Lord, before You are: my heart, my tongue, my senses and my members;
they strive indeed, but of themselves they cannot.
Do You that which they cannot, and perfect the desire of me, a sinner,
You who are accustomed to grant the just their desire. Amen.

ref: The Star and the Three Kings · Matthew 2:1–2
text: O good Jesus, born of the Virgin,
who revealed Yourself to the Magi with a star to lead them,
and made them return to their own country by another way:
merciful Jesus, let the light of Your grace shine upon the darkness of my conscience,
and by Your joyful appearing, give me full knowledge of You and of myself,
that within I may see You, within I may find You;
and there offer to Your majesty the myrrh of inward compunction,
the incense of devout prayer,
the gold of holy love;
and that I, who by the way of darkness and of guilt withdrew
from the homeland of supernal happiness,
may return to it by the way of truth and grace, with You to lead me. Amen.

ref: The Light to the Nations · Luke 2:22–32
text: O Jesus, to be desired,
who in the temple gave yourself mercifully to just Simeon,
who longed to see you, that he might hold you:
come, sweetest Jesus,
and to me who await you with all my desire, grant yourself kindly.
Whatever impurity you find in me, drive out by purifying grace;
and make my heart your temple, deigning to dwell there;
there let me embrace and hold you in the arms of desire.
Grant me always to desire you, the fountain of light, who are with the Father;
and let me not go out of this life
until I see you with the eyes of my heart —
you who are the love and the desire,
the life and the reward of those who desire you.
Amen.

ref: The Flight into Egypt · Matthew 2:13–14
text: Lord Jesus Christ, who, while yet an infant, willed to suffer persecutions and exile,
and willed that infants then be slain for Your sake:
grant to me, wretched one, to bear all these things patiently for You,
and even death, if it must be;
to despise all the prosperity of the world, and to dread none of its adversity.
And you, O most holy Innocents, flowers and first-fruits of the martyrs,
who by innocence and the palm of martyrdom clung to the infant Son of God:
deign, out of the fullness of your grace and innocence,
to obtain for the weakness and misery of my sins
pardon and grace from that same most kind Son of God. Amen.

ref: The Return from Egypt · Matthew 2:19–21
text: O most gentle Jesus,
who, born of the Virgin, withdrew into the regions of Egypt,
and, called out of Egypt, returned into the land of Israel:
call me back, O Lord, your servant,
cast out from before the sight of your eyes into the Egypt of this exile,
banished in the darkness of this pilgrimage.
From the regions of Egypt, from the darkness of worldly living,
lead me back in body, lead me back in mind, lead me back in will.
Make me forsake my vices, and pass over to the virtues,
and deign to bring me into the land of promise:
now by faith, hope, and charity;
but in time to come, in reality, in vision, in glory, and in truth. Amen.

ref: The Boy Lost and Found · Luke 2:40–46
text: Lord Jesus Christ, Son of the living God,
who for three days were sought by your grieving parents,
and at last were found in the Temple:
grant to me, a wretch, to desire you,
by desiring to seek you,
by seeking to find you,
by finding to love you,
by loving to redeem my evils,
and, once redeemed, to do them no more.
You who give freely to him that asks,
are found by him that seeks,
are opened to him that knocks:
deny not to me, the least, what you promise to all.
And you who, returning to Nazareth at your parents' will,
and made subject to them, showed the pattern of obedience:
grant to me, hardened, to break my own will,
that I may be subject to you,
and, for your sake, to every human creature. Amen.

ref: The Hidden Years · Luke 2:51
text: O Jesus, meek, the very pattern of true patience,
and the example of humility,
drive far from me all swelling of pride,
and every appetite of vain glory,
every kind at once of so great a plague
and so great a malice.
Let there not be, O Lord, nor even seem to be,
any sign of so great an evil and so great a ruin
in Your servant —
not in my conduct, not in my words,
not in my deeds,
nor before You in my thoughts.
Found me in true and deep humility,
that no place may lie open in me
to the snares of my enemies,
and grant that I may be little in my own eyes,
that I may find full grace
in the eyes of Your majesty. Amen.

ref: The Voice in the Wilderness · Matthew 3:1–10; Luke 3:1–14
text: Most blessed John the Baptist, forerunner of Christ and most holy virgin,
who preached penance to sinners by your word
and showed it no less by your example,
leading a life austere in food and clothing,
and withdrawing yourself from the enticements of the world:
I beseech you, obtain for me by your holy prayers
that the Lord may grant me fitting abstinence
of food and drink, of thought, of speech, and of deed;
that He may guard me from all defilement of mind and body,
and grant me, as long as I dwell in this temporal life,
to be set apart from vices, and to do battle for Him,
and so to bring forth fruits worthy of penance,
that I may obtain pardon of all my sins
and come to life everlasting. Amen.

ref: The True Light · John 1:6–12
text: Lord Jesus Christ, Son of the living God, who art the true Light that enlightens every man coming into this world,
I, a wretch obscured by dense darkness, adore thee,
beseeching thy mercy, that thou wouldst enlighten my soul, form my mind, order my thoughts,
compose my senses, and direct my words and my deeds,
so that the author of iniquity and the lover of darkness may not snatch me away, nor find his mark in me;
but, struck by the splendour of the truest Light, may flee far from me,
that, pressing on by the clear and straight path toward thee, the Author of light,
I may come to thy everlasting glory. Amen.

ref: I Am Not the Christ · John 1:19–21
text: Lord, Lord, open to us, for we stand knocking at your door.
We dare not call ourselves yours if we do not do your works — for that, as your servant says, would be a lie.
You have said: You are my friends, if you do what I command you; do not say to us, I do not know you.
Give us the humility of John, than which nothing is greater,
who confessed he was not the Christ, and was not lifted up into pride.
Number us, who humbly acknowledge our own weakness, among the members of your body;
let us never be cut off from you by seizing a glory that is not ours.
Make us not air that lifts itself up, but earth that is humbled;
not the voice that claims to be the Word, but only a voice that prepares your way.

ref: The Tribunal of the Heart · Luke 11:32 (the men of Nineveh); cf. Proverbs 18:3, Psalm 130:1
text: Lord, the patience of your mercy has led me to repentance:
let me not waste it by delay.
You promised pardon to the one who repents,
but to the one who puts it off you did not promise tomorrow.
So I will not despise, I will not despair —
out of the depth of my sins I cry to you.
Receive the blood of a confessing soul, poured out in tears.
Judge me now within myself, that I be not judged by you;
turn my will and my ways toward the better while I still can.
I let go what is uncertain, and I hold fast to you, who are certain.
Grant me to do true penance while I am whole,
and to be found running toward you on my last day.

ref: The Lord Goes Down to Jordan · Matthew 3:13–15 (with Mark 1, Luke 3)
text: Will you be baptized, Lord Jesus?
For what, or what need have you of Baptism?
Does the healthy need medicine, or the clean need cleansing?
Whence comes sin to you, that Baptism should be needful?
What stain can the Lamb without stain bear?
What are you doing, Lord?
You are above the Son of the King, and below the Son of the King,
and nowhere do you show the royal scepter.
Show your dignity. Why alone, so lowly?
O humility of the power of Christ!
O sublimity of humility!
How you confound the folly of my vanity!
I know some little thing, or seem to myself to know more,
and already I cannot keep silent,
thrusting myself forward shamelessly,
quick to speak, swift to teach, slow to hear.
And Christ kept silent so long,
and so hid himself away.

ref: The Desert of Forty Days · Matthew 4:1–2; Luke 4:1–2
text: Lord Jesus, You went into the desert not for Your own need, but for ours.
Lead me by Your Holy Spirit, and not by any feigning of the evil one,
into the desert of my own heart and of quiet contemplation,
that, resting from the noise of the world, I may better wait upon God.
Teach me to crucify my flesh with its desires,
and, leaving the world behind in my mind,
to hunger for the eternal joys alone, as for the manna of the desert.
Prepare my soul for temptation, and gird me, in the bright days and the dark nights alike,
against the enemy who never ceases to assail me.
Amen.

ref: Behold the Lamb of God · John 1:29–34
text: O Lamb of God,
acknowledge me, a wretched man,
among the sheep to be set at your right hand.
But first forgive me my sins and offenses,
that you may the better acknowledge me among your sheep.
Lamb of God, have mercy on me.
Lamb of God, have mercy on me.
Lamb of God, give me peace.

ref: Come and See · John 1:35–39
text: O good Jesus, Redeemer of the lost, Saviour of the redeemed, hope of the exiled.
Sweet consolation of the poor in spirit, strength of the labouring, refreshment of the weary, crown of those who triumph.
The one reward of the citizens above, and their true gladness.
Renowned offspring of the most high God, and fruit of the virginal womb.
Fountain of all graces, of whose fullness we all have received.
Let me be borne toward You as the end of all things, reaching out, and hoping, and loving.
O Jesus, my desire! to You may I be led, You may I follow.
For You alone suffice, You alone save.
You alone are good and sweet to those who seek You and love Your name. Amen.

ref: The Water Made Wine · John 2:1–4
text: Lord Jesus Christ,
who on the third day — that is, in the time of grace —
came in the flesh to the wedding,
the joining of Thyself and Thy Church,
and didst turn water into wine,
showing that the things which in the old Scripture seemed carnal are spiritual:
Turn, I beseech Thee, my cold soul into the fervor of Thy love;
my tasteless soul, into the savor of Thy sweetness;
my unsteady, wavering soul, into the constancy of Thy strength and grace.
Change the water of all my snow-cold lukewarmness
into the wine of fruitful compunction,
that I, wretched as I am, may drink of it and be made drunk,
and at the last, of Thy mercy, vouchsafe to turn it into the wine of gladness. Amen.

ref: The Cleansing of the Temple · John 2:13–17
text: Brethren, let every Christian, in the members of Christ, be eaten up with zeal for the house of God.
Who is eaten up with zeal for the house of God?
He who, whatever he sees there gone wrong, strives to have it corrected, longs to see it amended, and is not at rest.
If you cannot amend it, bear it, groan, sigh, endure the chaff.
You see a brother running to the theatre? Forbid him, warn him, grieve over him — so the zeal of the Lord's house eats you up.
You see others running, and willing to be drunk? Forbid whom you can, hold whom you can, frighten whom you can.
But if you are cold and withered, looking to yourself alone,
and say in your heart: What is it to me to care for the sins of others? My own soul is enough, that I keep it whole for God —
does there not come to your mind that servant who hid his talent, and would not put it out?
Was he not accused, not because he lost it, but because he kept it without gain?
So then hear, my brethren, that you be not at rest:
cease not to gain souls for Christ, for you yourselves were gained by Christ.

ref: He Must Increase, I Must Decrease · John 3:22–30 (with Matthew 14, Mark 6)
text: Lord Jesus Christ, good Master,
teach me the truth of life, of justice, and of doctrine —
of heart, of mouth, and of work —
never to forsake it through fear, or human favor, or worldly regard.
Let no tenderness of carnal fear or love prevail in me
to hinder the perfection of spiritual love.
And because, through the sin of our first parents,
our irascible power has contracted weakness, our rational power darkness, and our concupiscible power a proneness to evil,
give me the Paraclete: a defender against persecutions,
an illuminator against errors, a kindler against concupiscence,
that in all things I may be able to do Your good pleasure, and to keep from offending You. Amen.

ref: The Light Goes Up Into Galilee · Matthew 4:12–17
text: Lord Jesus Christ,
who, having compassion on our misery, and as a true physician willing to give us the medicine of souls,
began your preaching from penance, and bade us, while we have time, to do it:
grant to me, a wretched sinner, the space and the effect of repenting —
you who have so long, in mercy, spared me the offender, for which be praise to you;
that through penance the kingdom of heaven may draw near and be unlocked to me,
which is set far off and shut by sin —
through true repentance and the compunction of tears.
Grant that I may be, in the moment of death, such as you made me to be in the water of regeneration, in the laver of Baptism,
that I may deserve to be fit for the kingdom of heaven. Amen.

ref: The Boat of the Obedient · Luke 5:1–6 (with Matthew 4)
text: Lord Jesus Christ,
make me rush upon the nets of preaching and of desire and of good work
through eagerness to hear the word of God;
to wash them clean of all avarice, flattery, and empty glory;
to draw back the boat of religion from earthly things,
to sit resting within it,
and to teach others by example,
and to lead into the deep of contemplation and of preaching;
to let down the nets at the word of Your inspiration,
and in the waters of tribulations
to enclose a copious multitude of inward consolations.
Call and draw me, a wretched man, to Your holy discipleship,
that, leaving all things and following You,
I may deserve to attain the fellowship of Your poor.
Amen.

ref: The Zeal of Christ in Preaching · Matthew 5 (with Luke 9:59–60)
text: Lord Jesus Christ,
who as a diligent physician went about seeking the sick,
and healed the infirmities of bodies and of souls,
heal me, I pray, within and without,
labouring under many infirmities;
draw me after You, that I may follow You,
passing over from vices to virtues,
keeping the Ten Commandments,
dwelling peaceably with others,
humbly confessing my sins,
and dreading the divine judgments.
Lead me out of the pit of misery and the mire of dregs,
and direct my steps into the way of salvation,
that I may advance in Your commandments,
and hasten to the blessed homeland,
and come there without hindrance. Amen.

ref: The Calling of Matthew · Matthew 9:9–13; Mark 2:14–17; Luke 5:27–32
text: Lord Jesus Christ, who in many ways callest and admonishest me to follow thee,
kindle my heart by thy divine inspiration,
that by thy grace I may follow thee who callest,
and that nothing may separate me from thy charity.
Grant that I may serve thee with holy thoughts, meditations, and affections,
with good works and virtues,
and may set before thee a feast
from the great delight and devotion of my mind.
And thou who willest mercy rather than sacrifice,
and camest to call sinners rather than the just,
grant to me, a wretched sinner,
that I may deserve to know thy mercy mercifully shown in me. Amen.

ref: The Choosing of the Twelve · Luke 6:12–13
text: Lord Jesus Christ,
who out of your inestimable mercy came into the world,
to call sinners back from their wanderings to repentance,
and of these were pleased to choose many to be your confidants and your own disciples:
recall me, O merciful God, a wretched sinner astray;
embrace me as I return,
strengthen me in my grief,
instruct me in my ignorance,
and at the last, admitting me, though unworthy, among your disciples,
lift me wholly from the desire of earthly things,
and carry my mind altogether to the things of heaven,
that I may hear and understand your words, and fulfil them all in deed. Amen.

ref: Upon the Mountain · Matthew 5:1–5
text: Lord Jesus Christ,
who, that You might teach the higher summits of the virtues,
ascended the mountain with Your disciples,
and there taught the blessednesses and the sublime virtues,
and promised to each its fitting reward:
grant to me, frail as I am,
that hearing Your voice,
I may strive through the exercise of the virtues to have merit,
so that, in Your mercy, I may obtain also the reward.
Make me, who consider the reward, not refuse the work of the reward,
but let the hope of eternal salvation soften in me the pain of the present remedy,
and kindle my soul to the brightness of the work.
Make me, wretched now, blessed —
blessed with the blessedness of the way through grace,
and at the last blessed with the blessedness of the homeland through glory.
Amen.

ref: Salt of the Earth, Light of the World · Matthew 5:13–17
text: Lord Jesus Christ, who to the men of old promised the things of time,
but to us things eternal, that our justice may overflow:
grant me to shine before You and before my neighbors in word and in deed,
and not to break Your Law, but to fulfill it beyond measure.
Keep me from anger and from offending my neighbor,
that the gift of heart, of mouth, or of good work which I offer You
may be acceptable in Your sight.
Grant me, most merciful God, to guard against evil desires and glances,
to turn away from every oath,
that as I abstain from wronging my neighbor, so also from wronging You,
and may please You in all things, always. Amen.

ref: The Other Cheek · Matthew 5:38–40 (with Luke 6:29)
text: Lord Jesus Christ, most gentle Master of all humility and patience,
grant to me, the least and simplest of Your servants,
that, setting myself low and beneath all others,
I may long to be despised by all, and trodden underfoot,
and that, bearing injury patiently in body or in goods,
I may keep a heart made ready to endure still more,
and may help, as far as I am able, all who ask of me in their bodily need.
Grant also that I may be able not only to love my friends,
but even my enemies — those who persecute me in heart, in word, or in deed —
to do them good, and to bless them, and to pray for them,
that by Your grace I may be counted worthy among Your sons and chosen ones. Amen.

ref: The Whip of Cords · John 2:13–17
text: Lord Jesus, let the zeal of your house consume me.
Make a whip even of my own sins,
and drive out from the temple of my heart all that I keep there for sale —
the doves I would not give freely,
the sheep's clothing over a wolf's mind,
the works I do for human praise and not for love of you.
Pour out and scatter my buying and bargaining,
overturn the tables where I count my gain,
and do not let my soul, which should be a house of prayer, become a house of trade.
Bind me now with the cords of correction,
that I be not bound in the end for damnation;
and let me not rest, but labor to win for you the souls you have won.
Amen.

ref: Our Father · Matthew 6:9
text: O Power, O Word of wondrous working!
Come now, my good Lord Jesus, Word of the Father,
complete Thou my prayer,
perfect Thou these words spoken by Thee and laid upon my mouth.
Perfect them, and say Amen; say, let it be done;
say to me as Thou saidst to that Syrophoenician woman:
Be it done to thee as thou wilt.
O sweet love of Jesus, O sweet truth,
O sweet Amen, O sweet word, 'let it be':
Be it done to me according to Thy word.
Let it be done. Amen.

ref: Treasure in Heaven · Matthew 6:19–21; Luke 12:32–33
text: Lord Jesus Christ,
make me lay up no earthly gain upon the earth,
but the rewards of merit in heaven;
and because no one can serve two masters with contrary allegiance,
free me from the dominion and servitude of the world, the flesh, and the devil,
that I may look toward heavenly contemplations, not earthly ones.
Add to the stature of my nature a cubit of grace in the present,
and of glory in the time to come,
that I may consider the lilies of the field, the devout of the Church,
clothed in the brightness of the virtues,
rather than the rich of this age, hay to be cast into the furnace of hell.
Let me seek above all the kingdom of God and His justice,
that with the provision of temporal things, along the way of the virtues,
I may come to the kingdom of heaven. Amen.

ref: Be Merciful, Judge Not · Matthew 7; Luke 6:36–38
text: Lord Jesus Christ,
who have taught us to show mercy to all, and to judge no one,
give me to fulfil what You teach,
that through You I may be able to please You.
You urge us to pray, and surely You will to give,
You who ever admonish us to ask.
I ask, then, because You command;
I seek, because You bid;
I knock, because You enjoin it.
You who made me ask, make me receive;
You who gave me to seek, grant me to find;
You who taught me to knock, open to the one who knocks, that he may enter in.
From You I have the desiring; from You may I have the obtaining.
Give what I may offer, guard what You require,
that You may will to crown what You bestow.
Amen.

ref: The Narrow Way · Matthew 7:13–14
text: Lord Jesus Christ, most merciful,
make me, by the narrow way of justice and the strait gate of penance, to enter into the hall of salvation and the life of glory.
Teach me to shun the deceit of the deceivers,
and grant me to imitate the simplicity and innocence of the spiritual sheep;
make me fix the root of my heart in heaven, not on earth,
that I may deserve to be found faithful not in the leaves of words only, but rather in the fruits of good works.
Make me do the will of the heavenly Father,
and, hearing Thy words, fulfill them in deed,
that, made firm in Thee, I may be parted from Thee by no temptations. Amen.

ref: The Healing of the Leper · Matthew 8:1–3 (with Mark 1 and Luke 5)
text: Lord Jesus Christ,
who came down from the mountain of the Father's throne
and from the virgin's womb
to heal the leprosy of the human race:
behold, I am a leper,
sprinkled with the many stains of my sins.
I adore you, Lord.
If you will, you can make me clean.
Stretch out the hand of your grace and tenderness;
touch the inward and the outward of the leper who calls upon you;
have mercy on me, a penitent,
and command the sickness of sin to depart.
My God and my mercy,
who do not will the death of the sinner,
but that he be converted and live:
accomplish this in me, a sinner,
that I may not die in my soul,
but be converted, and live with you forever. Amen.

ref: Speak But the Word · Matthew 8:5–10; Luke 7:1–9
text: Lord Jesus Christ, I am not worthy that you should enter under the roof of my flesh,
for the boy of my sensuality lies paralysed, undone by the sickness of sin in the house of my body,
and is grievously tormented by the stirrings of its desires.
But come by the inpouring of your grace, and speak the word of truth,
that my boy may be healed, and rise again from his sins.
Grant, merciful God, to me — a wretch set under the power of your grace,
and holding from your grace the natural strengths and powers under me —
that I may have the power to drive off harmful thoughts and motions,
to abide in what is good, and to command my body to serve the spirit. Amen.

ref: The Unclean Spirit Silenced · Mark 1:21–28; Luke 4:31–37
text: Lord Jesus Christ, remove and cast out the unclean spirit from me,
that it may in no way be able to stain me, or to hold me fast in stains;
that I may be, and may appear, dead to the world,
once I am freed from that depraved possessor who drives us on through unclean things.
I ask You also, Lord, physician of souls, that You would deign to heal me from the fever of vices,
so that, rising up from them, I may be able to minister to You and to render You a pleasing service.
Heal me too, Lord, and all who are held by the varied languors of the soul,
that, made sound and whole from our sins, we may minister to You and accomplish what is well-pleasing to You. Amen.

ref: The Widow of Nain · Luke 7:11–16
text: Lord Jesus Christ, come into Nain, into my soul tossed by temptations;
draw near to the gate, holding back the senses, that they may not pass into the affections;
come by grace, touch the heart by correction;
let the bearers — the occasions and opportunities of sinning — stand still;
say to the soul lying in its sins that it may sit up by good will,
begin to speak by confession,
and arise by good works;
give it back to its mother, the nurse of grace, that it may stand fast in steadfastness;
and so may Your truth visit Your people — the powers of my soul, its affections and thoughts — by knowledge,
Your power by working, and Your goodness by keeping me. Amen.

ref: No Place to Lay His Head · Matthew 8:19–22; Luke 9:57–60
text: Lord Jesus Christ, good Master, searcher of hearts, and knower of secrets,
shut out and renew from me all craftiness and pretence,
and make me thy true and faithful disciple,
and without feigning to follow thee, the truthful Master.
Grant also that I may not put off following thee for any carnal affection,
nor prefer the lesser good to the greater.
Grant me, Lord my God, that putting my hand to the plough of penance,
and purposing to follow thee through a state of better life,
I may not look back — in deed or in purpose — by returning to my former state,
lest I be made unfit for the obtaining of the kingdom of God. Amen.

ref: The Master Asleep in the Boat · Matthew 8:23–27
text: Lord Jesus Christ,
command the blasts and motions of suggestions and temptations;
come, and walk upon the waves of my heart,
that all within me may become serene and tranquil.
Let my heart rest in You, my God;
let my heart, a great sea swelling with waves,
sleep to all things that are beneath heaven,
and keep watch in You,
that I may embrace You, my one good,
and contemplate You, the light of my eyes,
and rejoicing may sing and say:
I sleep, and my heart keeps watch;
and that other word: In peace, in the selfsame, I will sleep and take my rest.
Amen.

ref: Legion · Matthew 8:28–32; Mark 5:1–13; Luke 8:26–33
text: Lord Jesus Christ, most kind lover of men,
I beseech thy goodness with tears:
free me from all the filth of my sins,
and from the assaults of the demons,
and, having freed me from them, mercifully keep me unharmed until the end,
that to thy glory and the good of my neighbours
I may be able to tell in words and show in works
what great things thou hast done for my soul, set free by thy grace;
so that others, knowing that this is the change of the right hand of the Most High,
may all be moved by my example,
and, by thy mercy, be converted to better things. Amen.

ref: Lowered Through the Roof · Matthew 9:1–8; Luke 5:18–26
text: Lord Jesus Christ,
who in Your Passion climbed into the little boat of the Cross,
in Your Resurrection crossed the water,
and in Your Ascension came into Your own city:
behold, the fear of my sins,
of the wrath of God,
of the danger of approaching weakness,
and of the uncertainty of death,
offers to You a soul loosened by the sickness of sin.
Speak to the one who lies silent in his sins,
that he may trust in the grace of forgiveness,
rise again through contrition and confession,
take up his bed through making amends,
and walk by the growth of the virtues into his home, eternal blessedness;
that the crowds of the faithful may see, and fear, and glorify God,
who has given such power for the good of men.
Amen.

ref: If I Only Touch the Hem · Luke 8:41–48 (with Matthew 9 and Mark 5)
text: Lord Jesus Christ, I adore the feet of Thy mercy and Thy truth.
Most loving One, I beseech Thee:
heal by the touch of Thy grace my soul,
bleeding and stained with manifold sins.
Raise it from the death of an evil and hidden will and purpose,
and restore me to God and Thy Father,
who hast adopted me as a son among Thy co-heirs.
Remember not, good Lord, Thy justice against Thy sinner,
nor Thy wrath against Thy guilty one;
but be mindful of Thy kindness toward Thy creature,
and of Thy compassion toward Thy wretched one,
O Lord my God. Amen.

ref: Son of David, Have Mercy · Matthew 9:27–34
text: Lord Jesus Christ, light of eternal brightness,
enlighten my inward eyes,
that I may never fall asleep in the death of the soul;
that, enlightened by Your grace,
I may see all that ought to be done,
and, helped by that same grace, grow strong to fulfil what I have seen;
and thence may recount Your benefits,
to Your glory and the good of others.
Open also, Lord, my mute mouth,
loosing my sins by the infusion of grace,
and freeing my tongue to Your praises;
that, having received the grace of speech,
I may accuse myself, praise You as God,
build up my neighbour, and proclaim the truth. Amen.

ref: The Harvest is Great · Matthew 9:36–10:5; Luke 10:2; 9:1–2
text: Lord Jesus Christ,
who, sending Your disciples out to preach,
gave them power to heal, and precepts for living and conversing:
direct my feet into the way of peace,
and give me health of mind and of body;
that I may have justice in my works,
discipline in my ways,
and in all things Your fear and Your love.
Mortify in me all vices,
and restore me, within and without, by Your gifts,
that the things You commanded Your disciples
I may, after my small measure, be worthy to imitate,
and with them, by Your mercy, be rewarded with eternal glory.
Amen.

ref: Sheep Among Wolves · Matthew 10:16–17
text: Most merciful Lord Jesus Christ,
who sent your disciples to do battle against the world and the devil,
as sheep among wolves,
and gave them consolation in the bearing of tribulations:
strengthen my frailty,
and grant me firmness against the perils of the world and the snares of the devil.
Guard me from my enemies, visible and invisible,
and give me patience in adversities and tribulations,
that I may always know you, my Creator, to be my Defender,
and being always defended by you in this present life,
may come after it to you, absolved of all my sins. Amen.

ref: Fear Not Those Who Kill the Body · Matthew 10:28
text: Lord Jesus Christ,
grant me so to despise all earthly desires and sinful delights
that I may fear to suffer nothing on earth for your name;
but let your name and your Passion be so inscribed forever upon my heart
that my soul may deserve to hold the palm of victory against all things adverse and prosperous,
and so confess you in heart, in word, and in deed before men on earth,
that you, according to your promise, may confess me before your Father in heaven,
where by your tenderness and mercy, with your saints and your elect,
I may deserve to receive the joys promised through you, good Jesus, to those who thus confess you. Amen.

ref: Fire Cast Upon the Earth · Luke 12:49–53; Matthew 10:34–37
text: Lord Jesus Christ, splendor of the Father's glory,
send into me the fire and fervor of the Holy Spirit,
that the love of God and of neighbor may be kindled, increased, and made perfect in me.
Grant me to leave behind entirely the affection of carnal kinship and the love of bodily delight,
and to love you above all things;
to hold consideration and discretion in all I do,
and never to trust in the security of my own strength or merits;
but always to send to you the embassy of tears and of good works,
and to seek peace from you and bear it away.
Grant me also to renounce all things in affection, and to leave all things in deed,
that I may be your true disciple. Amen.

ref: The Cup of Cold Water · Matthew 10:40–42
text: Lord Jesus Christ, good Master,
grant me so to venerate and receive, for your name's sake,
the prophets, who preach you by their teaching,
and the just, who show you forth by a good life,
and your disciples and messengers, who in any way proclaim you;
and to render them the services and good offices of love,
that I may merit, by your gift, to obtain a reward together with them, through their merits.
Grant me also, Lord my God,
reverently to hear prelates, preachers, and priests,
and by obeying to acquiesce in their admonitions,
that with the truly obedient I may be able to find grace before you.
Amen.

ref: Are You the One? · Matthew 11:2–6
text: Lord Jesus Christ,
since you are he who is to come to save us,
and we look for no other:
grant that, bound by your love and fear,
we may come out of the reed-bed of vanity
and out of the soft clothing of pleasure,
into the desert of repentance.
Declare to us, by the working of your mercy,
that you are the truth, by which the inwardly blind see;
the charity, by which the lame walk;
the humility, by which the proud lepers are cleansed;
the word, by which the deaf hear;
the life, by which the dead rise again;
and the power, by which the poor have the gospel preached to them —
that all may be converted to you. Amen.

ref: The Children in the Marketplace · Matthew 11:16–19
text: Lord Jesus Christ,
who by word and example have called us
to grieve for our sins and to rejoice for spiritual goods:
give me the grace of tears,
and water the dry ground of my heart with the dew of heaven,
that I may weep for myself every day of my whole life;
so that my tears may become my bread day and night,
and my mind, forgetful of vanity and misery, may burn in your love;
so that, dancing toward good works and rising to the praises of God,
I may now exult in hope,
and at the last, praising you forever, may rejoice with you without end.
Amen.

ref: Return and Rest · Luke 10:1–17 (with Mark 6:30–31)
text: Lord Jesus Christ,
grant me, through the holy preaching of thy disciples,
so to be taught by thy grace,
that I may delight to glory not in vanities,
nor in anything that breeds exaltation and self-importance in me,
but in thy cross and in all things that stir up humility within me.
May that holy Gospel teaching ever confirm and strengthen my heart
in profound humility and in every virtue,
that, cleansed from vices and adorned with virtues,
I may deserve to find rest for my soul, in this present life and in the life to come;
for my heart is restless until it rest in thee, O good Jesus. Amen.

ref: Sorrowful Unto Death · Matthew 26:36–39; Luke 22:41–44
text: Lord Jesus Christ, who for me, a wretch, willed to be sorrowful and heavy:
grant me to bear all my sorrows unceasingly up to You;
and deign to bear them together with me, in union with Your own sorrow.
O Jesus, who in prayer willed to be strengthened by an angel:
grant me, through the power of Your prayer,
that as I pray Your angel may ever stand by me, strengthening me.

ref: The Tears of the Magdalene · Luke 7:36–38
text: Lord Jesus, what she shamefully used for the odor of her flesh,
this she now offered to Thee in praise.
With my eyes I have sought Thee upon the earth.
Touched with sorrow of heart within, I run, contrite, to the fountain of Thy mercy.
I dare not set myself before Thee, for the shame of my sins;
behind, beside Thy feet, I take my place,
for I follow now the footsteps of Him whom I once assailed.
Wash me with the tears Thou hast given;
and let me not be satisfied with loving Thee.

ref: The Better Part · Luke 10:38–42
text: Lord Jesus Christ, who for us camest in the flesh,
through the union of the divine nature and the human,
let the bowels of thy mercy be moved over me,
thine unworthy servant, a sinner and guilty.
By the faith, the merits, and the prayers of that Martha,
who received thee as a guest in her virginal womb,
and of that Mary who kept thy words, pondering them in her heart —
of that most merciful Mother of thine —
come into my mind through the infusion of grace:
that I may love nothing but thee,
seek nothing, and desire to think of nothing but thee;
but that thou alone mayest be to me my hope and my every work.
I pray that even here I may in some measure taste beforehand
the sweetness of thy consolation. Amen.

ref: Have Mercy on Us, Son of David · Matthew 9:27–34
text: Lord Jesus Christ, light of eternal brightness,
illumine my inner eyes,
that I may never fall asleep in the death of the soul;
that, enlightened by Your grace,
I may see all that must be done,
and, helped by that same grace, grow strong to fulfill what I have seen;
and from there tell out Your benefits, to Your glory and the good of others.
Open also, Lord, my mute mouth,
loosing my sins by the pouring-in of grace,
and freeing my tongue to Your praises;
that, receiving the grace of speech,
I may accuse myself, praise You as God,
build up my neighbor, and preach the truth. Amen.

ref: The Word That Heals from Afar · John 4:46–53
text: Lord Jesus Christ,
who from Judea of angelic confession and praise came into Galilee, the wheel of this world:
for I am sick, tempted by the beauty of temporal things,
and so I begin to die, worn out by temptation.
Have mercy on me before I die the death of guilt, or of the body, or of hell,
that by the power of your word, by the humility of confession,
by fasting, by prayer, by mercy, and by example,
through your grace I may be freed from the fever of concupiscence and the sickness of sin,
and may yield to no disordered passion. Amen.

ref: The Sower in the Boat · Matthew 13:1–9; Mark 4:1–9; Luke 8:4–8
text: Lord Jesus Christ, make me to go out from my old life,
that the seed of your Word be not trampled on the wayside of distraction,
nor wither on the rock of hard obstinacy,
nor be choked among the thorns of anxious care;
but rather, in the good and best ground of a humble, compassionate, and joyful heart,
may it bring forth the hundredfold fruit in patience.
Grant me also to understand and to do all these things spoken in parables,
and by word, or at least by example, to teach them to others. Amen.

ref: Today This Scripture Is Fulfilled · Luke 4:16–21
text: Lord Jesus Christ,
who so humbled yourself to every service
that you did not disdain to take up the office of reader,
and at last, after the gift of your teaching humbly poured out upon the men of Nazareth,
you patiently received evil for good,
when you permitted yourself to be led without resistance
by those who wished to cast you down:
give me, good Jesus, this grace —
that, imitating you, I may long to bend myself to every service of humility,
and offer myself gladly and ready;
grant me also to bear injuries patiently,
and to seek no vengeance for them,
and to love all my adversaries from the heart,
and to show them kindness. Amen.

ref: A Lamp Extinguished · Matthew 14:6–12; Mark 6:21–29
text: My Lord and my God,
into your hands I commend my spirit.
As John, friend of the Bridegroom, gave his life for the truth,
so let me not be ashamed of the truth,
nor wear gladness within while feigning grief without.
Quench in me the lustful will before it becomes lust,
and curb the lust before it sinks me into deeper guilt.
Grant me to come swiftly to you, and to follow reverently,
passing over, by this office of love, into your fellowship.
My Lord and my God, into your hands I commend my spirit.

ref: The Five Loaves · Matthew 14:13–21; John 6:1–14
text: Lord Jesus Christ,
make me lift the eyes of my mind from carnal delights,
from earthly riches and worldly ambitions, up to You,
and make me sit down upon the grass
of fleshly pleasure, of passing possession, of worldly glory,
that I may be worthy to be filled
with the refreshment of the five spiritual loaves:
the fear of the divine judgment, the horror of sin,
the sorrow of contrition, the shame of confession,
and the labor of satisfaction;
and with the two fishes:
steadfastness of purpose,
and the desire to change and grow toward the better —
which one boy, namely humility, holds,
the humility that is wont to merit grace and glory.
Amen.

ref: The Lord Who Fled the Crown · John 6:15 (cf. Matthew 14:22–23)
text: Lord Jesus Christ, you who resist the proud and give grace to the humble,
grant to all who take up the state of dignity and rule for the good of obedience, humbly and without ambition, and so persevere in it,
grace in the present, and glory in time to come;
and resist the ambitious, hedging their ways with thorns, that they may not prosper in them,
so that they neither delight to preside over men, nor rejoice in the singularity of their honor,
and that at last, enlightened and returned to their heart, they may desist from the deadly vice of ambition,
and cease to be such, that — by your mercy — they may not perish forever. Amen.

ref: On the Waves of the Night · Matthew 14:24–31 (with Mark 6, John 6)
text: Lord Jesus Christ, most merciful,
deign to climb into the little boat of my heart,
and still the windy gusts of pride and the storms of rising vices,
that no wind of temptation may overturn me, nor any wave drown me.
Give me counsel in disturbance,
help in persecution,
consolation in tribulation,
fortitude in adversity,
and strength in every temptation.
Free me from the tempests of this stormy sea,
and lead me to the tranquillity of the quiet shore,
granting me now the peace of time and of heart,
and after this, the peace of eternity. Amen.

ref: Seek Jesus for His Own Sake · John 6:22–27
text: Lord Jesus Christ, sufficient salvation of my soul,
grant me to desire You alone,
desiring You for Your own sake;
to seek You, not for any other thing;
seeking, to find You;
finding, to hold You;
holding, to love You;
loving, to redeem my sins;
and the sins redeemed, not to do again.
Enlighten, I beg You, Lord, my heart with the light of Your divine grace,
that I may have You as my guide in all my ways,
and that I may ever fear and love You above all things, who are above all things,
and do Your will in all things, and never depart from You,
but always cling to You,
for You alone are enough, and You promise life everlasting,
to which may You mercifully bring me. Amen.

ref: Lord of the Sabbath · Matthew 12:1–8 (with Mark 2:23–28, Luke 6:1–5)
text: Lord Jesus, you are greater than the temple, for you are its Lord;
you are Lord even of the sabbath.
You will the affection of mercy and of pity
more than the ceremonies of the old Law;
for the sacrifice pleasing to God is the salvation of men.
Set me free from terthings to which my heart has clung,
that the chaff be parted from the grain within me,
and so feed the hunger of your Church.
Amen.

ref: The Withered Hand · Luke 6:6–10 (with Matthew 12 and Mark 3)
text: Lord Jesus Christ,
I beg you, by the depths of your mercy,
that you would mercifully heal me,
withered and powerless to do good works,
and make me strong and fit for every good work.
Make me stand in the midst of the virtues,
that, at your command and by your working,
I may stretch out my hands to justice and not to iniquity,
and, exercising myself in good,
may do your good pleasure in all things;
and, withering away in evil,
may wholly abstain from offending you.
Grant me, good Jesus,
that above all the riches and delights of this world
I may attend to the word of God,
and to the salvation of myself and of others.
Amen.

ref: The Blind and Mute Man · Matthew 12:22; Luke 11:14
text: Lord Jesus,
the devil I led into the house of my soul:
drive him out, and dwell there Yourself.
Open my tongue, that I may praise You,
build up my neighbor,
and accuse myself before You in confession.
Restore my light, that I may see behind me
my sins, how foul, how many, how grievous, and grieve;
and see before me Your judgment to come, and fear;
and unseal my hearing, that I may hear You
warning, doing good, inspiring, threatening.
Cast out the demon, and let me see and speak.
Amen.

ref: The Sign of Jonah · Matthew 12:38–41
text: Lord Jesus Christ, good Master, work in me the sign of Thy grace unto good,
that me, a wretched man, whom that great whale and dragon has swallowed up by sins spiritual and carnal, as by days and nights, and these three, by consent, by deed, and by habit,
Thou wouldst mercifully deign to deliver from his jaws.
Make me also to bewail my past sins, and to beware of those to come.
Dry me from the moisture of concupiscence, that it may find no rest in me;
cleanse me with the brooms of confession from the filth of my vices, and adorn me with true virtues:
lest he find me empty of good things, and make me to sin again. Amen.

ref: Only Touch the Hem · Luke 8:43–48 (with Matthew 9:20–22; Mark 5:25–34)
text: Lord Jesus Christ, I adore the feet of Thy mercy and Thy truth:
I beseech Thee, most loving One, heal by the touch of Thy grace my soul, bloodied and stained with manifold sins.
Raise it up from the death of an evil and hidden will and purpose,
and restore it to God Thy Father, to whom Thou hast adopted me as a son among Thy co-heirs.
Remember not, good Lord, Thy justice against Thy sinner,
nor Thy wrath against Thy guilty one;
but be mindful of Thy kindness toward Thy creature,
and of Thy mercy toward Thy wretched one, O Lord my God. Amen.

ref: Tear Down the Barns · Luke 12:13–21
text: Lord Jesus, you would not be a judge of inheritance or a divider of estates,
for you came to gather, not to scatter,
to make many to have one heart and one soul.
You are not the God of dissension, but of gathering, and of peace, and of unity.
Take from me, then, all covetousness, inward and outward,
the greedy seeking and the tight holding of passing things.
Teach me that my life does not consist in the abundance of what I possess,
and that earthly possession is not mine, but heavenly.
Let me not build greater barns,
but scatter, giving to the poor, who are your storehouses;
for of all I have, mercy alone will go with me.
Amen.

ref: The Rich Fool and His Barns · Luke 12:13–18
text: Lord Jesus, You who are not the God of division, but of gathering, of peace, and of unity,
You who came to make men of one heart and one soul, that they might hold all things in common:
turn me away from the earthly, for whose sake You stooped to descend.
Let me not reckon mine the goods I cannot carry away with me.
Teach me that a man's possession is not earthly, but heavenly,
and that the true riches of a man are spiritual, not of the earth.
Open in me the barns that are the poor of Christ:
that I may not hoard, but scatter and give. Amen.

ref: The Pool of Bethesda · John 5:1–6
text: Lord Jesus Christ,
heal me, languishing, worn down by a long and evil sickness,
and paralytic, weighed down by a lasting numbness toward the good.
Grant me that I may rise, withdrawing from sin,
and take up my pallet, bearing the burden of penance for it,
and walk, going forward in the good,
from good to better, from virtue to virtue.
And being made whole, by Thy gift,
may I strive to abstain from sins henceforth,
lest something worse befall me, here or in time to come.
Grant me also, good Jesus, that following Thee in Thy humility,
I may despise human and earthly glory,
and not desire to be praised or lifted above others,
but seek the glory of Thee alone,
and long to please Thee alone. Amen.

ref: The Barren Tree · Luke 13:1–9
text: Lord Jesus Christ,
grant me to do the fruit of good works fervently,
and to persevere in them without ceasing,
lest, being found without fruit, I deserve to be cut down and cast into the fire.
Grant me also, my God,
that I be not bent downward through sin,
but look upward through righteousness;
nor, curved away from the uprightness of mind,
think upon and love things earthly and passing,
but lift the eyes of my mind to the contemplation of things heavenly,
and seek the riches of eternal blessedness.
O Lord, look upon me through Your mercy;
call me through inward inspiration;
heal me through the remission of sin;
touch me through the sorrow of making amends;
raise me to the things above through the fervor of love.
Amen.

ref: The Man Swollen with Dropsy · Luke 14:1–11
text: Lord Jesus Christ, take hold of me and protect me with the hand of your mercy,
lest the dropsy of carnal pleasure, or avarice, or pride, prevail in me;
and grant me, through a true humility of heart and mouth and work,
to recline in the lowest place, reckoning myself less than others,
and through mercy not to neglect to invite the needy,
succoring their necessities according to my power.
Most bountiful Giver, O God, grant to me, a wretched man,
to eat the heavenly bread, which is you yourself, in the kingdom of God;
for which bread, O Lord, we daily do battle for you,
for which all the day long we are mortified, that we may live in your life. Amen.

ref: The Great Supper · Luke 14:16–24
text: Lord Jesus Christ,
who, willing that all men be saved,
have prepared for all the refreshment of heavenly blessedness,
and have called many to it in many ways:
do not shut me out, wretched as I am,
from that general grace which you came to impart to all;
and grant me to trample down all pride and ambition,
avarice and greed,
the lust of the flesh and its pleasure,
that these, or any other hindrances,
may not bar the eternal banquet against me;
but, I who am poor in grace and in virtue,
and weak in good works,
and blind in true knowledge,
and lame in right affection —
mercifully lead me into that very feast. Amen.

ref: The Feast of Tabernacles · John 7:1–8
text: Lord Jesus Christ,
grant me with heart and desire to go up to the festal day of the eternal solemnity,
and always to prepare myself for it,
that when the time of your visitation shall come,
I may deserve to arrive there happily, to contemplate you face to face.
O fountain of living water,
I thirst and I long, wretched as I am, for your grace;
I come and I sigh to you, seeking it with my whole heart.
Give me, then, to drink,
and to receive of it so abundantly
that the gifts of graces may both suffice for me
and flow from me to my neighbors through good will, and profit them;
so that you, seeing in me the work of mercy,
may be moved to pour a fuller grace upon me. Amen.

ref: The Woman Taken in Adultery · John 8:1-11
text: O merciful Jesus,
who didst mercifully free the woman taken in adultery from her accusers,
and, leaving her uncondemned, didst send her away in peace:
before thee stands my soul, an adulteress,
who has departed from thee, the true Spouse, as often as she consented to the suggestions of the adulterous enemy.
My conscience accuses me; my works and wicked deeds accuse me.
Enter not, O Lord, into judgment with her,
nor remember her ancient iniquities;
free the sinner and the guilty one from her accusers;
dismiss in peace her conscience, absolved from thy dread judgment,
for it is proper to thee always to have mercy and to spare,
and there is no number to thy mercy. Amen.

ref: Light of the World · John 8:12, 28, 31–34
text: Lord Jesus Christ, who invite us to hear the word of God,
teach us to bear insults and injuries,
not to seek our own glory,
not to forsake the truth of life, of justice, and of doctrine to avoid scandal,
not to cease from preaching because of insult and injury repeated.
Jesus Christ, highest truth, goodness, justice, mercy,
generosity, purity, sobriety, humility, charity,
you who are stoned and hidden from the lying and the malicious,
the unjust, the impious, the greedy, the unclean, the lustful, the proud, and the idle:
do not go out from the temple of my soul;
make me correctable, and in all things at one with you. Amen.

ref: The Man Born Blind · John 9:1–28
text: Lord Jesus Christ, who gave light to the eyes of the man born blind,
give light, I beseech you, to the eyes of my heart,
that I may not stumble in the darkness, nor ever fall asleep in death.
O God of my life, how vainly my days have been spent,
how fruitlessly the times have slipped away that you gave me to do your will in them — and I did it not!
How many years, how many months, how many days, how many hours have perished with me,
in which I lived before you without fruit!
Grant, O Father to be loved, that this remnant of my time be fruitful and made holy in your grace,
that in the days of eternity it may find a place, and be counted worthy before you. Amen.

ref: The Door of the Sheepfold · John 10:1–4
text: Lord Jesus Christ, good Shepherd,
who for Thy sheep didst lay down Thy life as their ransom,
Thy flesh for their food, and Thy blood for their drink;
who wast made for us the door into the Church militant and triumphant,
that through Thee we might enter, to be saved, in unto Thee:
know me among Thy sheep,
and look upon us in mercy, directing us into the way of salvation,
that we may know Thee, and be conformed to Thee by imitating Thee.
Let us not hear the voice of strangers — of the world, the flesh, and the devil —
but Thine alone, obeying Thy commandments and Thy counsels,
that we may have the life of grace, and more abundantly the life of glory,
and may find with Thee the pastures of eternal refreshment.
Amen.

ref: In the Porch of Solomon · John 10:22-30
text: Take care, then, that you also,
while winter draws near,
while this present life is shaken by the storms of iniquity,
may celebrate the spiritual dedication of your own temple,
ever renewing yourself,
and setting ascensions in your heart.
Then Jesus will be present in the porch of Solomon,
granting you a state of peace under his own governing.
For in the world to come,
no one will be able to accomplish the solemnities of renewal.

ref: The Washing of Hands · Matthew 15:1–8 (with Mark 7)
text: Lord Jesus Christ, grant me so to keep the commandments of God without transgression
that I may set them before all things, one and all,
and depart from them on no occasion whatever.
Grant me also, in all that enters the mouth of my body, to resist gluttony,
and to strive above all after the inner cleanness of the heart,
and to guard diligently the mouth of my heart and of my body.
And because human watchfulness does not suffice unless the divine be present,
therefore, Lord, I humbly beg you:
set a guard over both these mouths of mine,
that nothing may enter or come forth from them
which can stain me spiritually in my soul, and before you.
Amen.

ref: The Crumbs Beneath the Table · Matthew 15:21–27
text: Lord Jesus Christ, I ask thee and beseech thee, have mercy on me,
and help me in my necessities, and in the vexations of temptations and tribulations.
And if, Lord, because I am a dog, and before thee scarcely even as a dog,
I am not worthy to receive from thee the whole bread — the greatness of thy gifts —
at least give me the little crumbs: some small portion of thy grace,
without which my soul is grievously vexed by the demon;
but with which, however small it be, it shall be set free from the demon.
For thy grace, dwelling in the soul, blots out all sins,
and makes the soul an adopted daughter to God. Amen.

ref: Ephphetha — Be Opened · Mark 7:31–35
text: Lord Jesus Christ, who at the last forsake the wicked,
come through the Sidon of preaching, to the sea of contrition,
of the Galilee of confession, of satisfaction, and of passing over,
amid the midst of the borders of the Decapolis of charity, advancing in the Decalogue,
and take us aside, apart from the crowd of manifold temptation.
Put the finger of discernment into the ears of our understanding,
and the taste of wisdom into the mouth of our affection,
that the bonds of our tongue may be loosed for confessing,
and that we may speak rightly, with word and deed not at odds with the will,
and that we may shun the praises of men,
so that the deaf may hear through conversion,
and the mute may speak through confession. Amen.

ref: Bread in the Wilderness · Matthew 15:29–36; Mark 8:1–6
text: Lord Jesus Christ, have mercy upon the multitude:
upon the penitent, and the just who are beginning, and those advancing, the perfect and the contemplative,
who through a three days' contrition, confession, and satisfaction,
through victory over the world, the flesh, and the devil,
through the bodily, the imaginary, and the spiritual vision,
await pardon, grace, and glory.
Refresh the first by watchful care, caution, and holy fear and desire.
Refresh the second by the spirit of fear, of piety, of knowledge, of fortitude, of counsel, of understanding, and of wisdom.
Refresh the third with the dowries of soul and body —
in present hope, which shall overflow in seven baskets in the blessedness to come. Amen.

ref: The One Bread in the Boat · Matthew 16:1–4; Mark 8:11–14
text: Lord Jesus Christ, work in me the sign of Your weakness and Your Passion unto good,
that thereby, through You, I may rejoice to be helped and consoled.
O Bread of life, grant me always to be refreshed by Your love,
that I may be less anxious for bread or for any temporal comfort.
Grant that I may beware of the leaven of perverse doctrine, of feigned justice, and of evil deed,
and that in these I may offend You neither actively nor passively.
O Splendor of the Father's glory, illumine my heart with the light of Your divine grace,
and draw me out of all errors and snares,
and direct me into the way of truth and justice and eternal salvation. Amen.

ref: Take Up Your Cross · Matthew 16:24; Luke 9:23
text: Lord Jesus Christ, grant me so to deny myself,
that in prosperity and in all things that delight for a time, I may turn away from evil, and seek nothing but Your honor;
and, renouncing my own will, that I may obey You, and for Your sake every creature.
Grant me also to take up my cross,
taking upon myself willingly afflictions and hardships,
and patiently bearing those laid upon me by others,
and having compassion on my neighbors in their needs,
and not ceasing to do good in every adversity.
Grant me also to follow You, imitating Your footsteps;
and, conforming myself to Your life, that I may come after You,
reaching at last the heavenly homeland. Amen.

ref: The Light on Tabor · Matthew 17:1–2; Luke 9:28–29
text: Lord Jesus, who upon the mountain were transfigured before Your disciples,
and made Your face to shine as the sun,
grant that, forsaking what is low and earthly,
and desiring what is high and heavenly,
we may dwell in mind among the things of heaven.
Let us not lie among the lowest pleasures,
but ever raise ourselves toward You.
Make us, who now firmly keep the faith of the Trinity,
to rejoice then in the eternal vision;
that, coming to the glory of the future resurrection,
we may be worthy to see You in Your beauty.
Amen.

ref: Servants of No Account · Matthew 17; Mark 9; Luke 9
text: Lord Jesus Christ, who taught us humility by word and example,
grant me that I may never think high things of myself, but always lowly things,
feel them in my heart, speak them with my mouth, and show them in my deeds.
Rescue me and keep me from the malignant spirits,
and defend me from all enemies, visible and invisible;
and grant me, O my Saviour and my God,
that, fittingly intent upon fasting and prayer,
I may, by Your help, overcome all the temptations of unclean spirits and of wicked men,
and may deserve, by Your mercy, to be freed from the enemies of mind and body,
and to be always safe. Amen.

ref: The Coin in the Fish's Mouth · Matthew 17:22–27; Mark 9:30–32; Luke 9:44–45
text: Lord Jesus Christ,
grant me to go to the sea,
calling my sins to mind in the bitterness of my soul;
and to cast the hook, in the contrition of my heart;
and with mouth opened, in confession,
to pay the coin, in satisfaction;
and so to be freed from the tribute of the devil and his exaction.
Grant me also, turned from my pride,
to become as a little one, lowly in my own eyes;
and so, through the narrow way and the strait gate,
may I be found worthy to enter into the kingdom of blessedness.
And the little ones too, and the lowly, may I receive for Your name's sake,
showing them the kindnesses of charity,
so that, receiving You in them,
I may be received by You in heaven. Amen.

ref: The Millstone and the Little Ones · Matthew 18:6–7; Mark 9:41
text: Lord Jesus Christ, good Master and ruler,
govern and rule mercifully all that is within me and without,
that never may scandal come from others to me, or from me to others;
but by your grace cut away and shut out from me every occasion of scandal.
Grant me also that I may presume to despise none of the little or the small ones,
but rather strive to honour them all —
those whom you so deigned to honour
that you appointed the blessed Angels to their service and keeping,
and you yourself, to save them, willed to come from heaven into the world and to die.
Amen.

ref: The Shepherd Who Carries Me Home · Luke 15:1–7
text: Come, Lord Jesus, seek your servant;
come, good Shepherd, seek your straying and weary sheep;
come, Spouse of Mother Church, seek the lost coin;
come, Father of mercy, receive the prodigal son returning to you.
Come, not with the rod, but with love, and in the spirit of gentleness;
come then, Lord, for you alone can call back the one who strays,
find the one who is lost, reconcile the one who has fled;
come, that you may work salvation on earth and joy in heaven;
and turn me to yourself,
and grant me to do true and perfect penance,
that I may be to the Angels an occasion of gladness,
O Lord God of my salvation. Amen.

ref: Seventy Times Seven · Matthew 18:15–17
text: Lord Jesus Christ,
grant me, unworthy as I am, in Your mercy,
that I may mend my sinning brother, correcting him with words in due season;
and if not by words, then at least by prayers and good examples, may I not cease to do this.
Grant me also that, abiding in the unity of the Church and in fraternal charity,
I may deserve to be heard in my prayers,
and to know You by Your favor and help in our midst,
You who have set Your dwelling among the peaceable.
Grant me this besides:
that as often as my brother shall sin against me, so often I may forgive him,
so that neither rancor within, nor any sign of rancor without, may be found in me in any way.
Amen.

ref: The King Who Settled Accounts · Matthew 18:23–28
text: We run after Thee, O Lord Jesus,
hearing that Thou dost not spurn the poor, nor abhor the sinner.
Thou didst not abhor the thief confessing Thee,
nor the woman weeping, nor the Canaanite woman pleading,
nor her taken in adultery, nor him sitting at the toll-booth,
nor the publican beseeching, nor the disciple denying,
nor the persecutor of Thy disciples, nor Thy very crucifiers.
So mighty art Thou at last to justify, as Thou art abundant to forgive.
The bride runs, the maidens run;
but she who loves more ardently runs more swiftly, and arrives the sooner.

ref: The Indissoluble Bond · Matthew 19:1–12
text: Lord Jesus Christ, who hast exhorted us to continence,
grant what Thou dost exhort,
and bestow on me, the frail one, chastity of mind and body;
and Thou who didst will the little children to come to Thee,
and laying Thy hands upon them didst bless them,
grant to me, a wretch, that I may be a little child in my own eyes,
so that I may find grace in the eyes of Thy majesty;
and what the little ones possess by nature, may I obtain in mercy by Thy grace,
that by the merits of all the little and humble ones who please Thee,
I, the last of all, with Thee as ruler, with Thee as guide,
may deserve to come to Thee,
and be rewarded by Thee with blessing in the heavens. Amen.

ref: What Do I Still Lack? · Matthew 19:16–20; Mark 10:17–20
text: Lord Jesus Christ, grant to me — wretched and frail,
who until now have kept Thy commandments negligently and badly —
grant me at least from now on to keep them,
that with those who keep them I may be worthy to enter into life.
Grant me also, most merciful Lord,
that, striving toward perfection,
I may despise all temporal things for Thy sake in my heart,
and relinquish them in deed;
and, having left all things, may follow Thee,
imitating Thee, and walking in Thy footsteps;
that, with Thee as ruler, with Thee as guide,
I may come rejoicing to Thee —
my treasure, desirable and unfailing —
and forever delight in the sight of Thee. Amen.

ref: The Guarded Gateway · Matthew 19:21; Luke 14:33
text: Let the fear of God be to you more forceful than any necessity.
For if you are always willing to set the occasions before yourself,
you will keep nothing of the things commanded,
and so you will trample underfoot all that is enjoined.
Lord Jesus, set this holy fear before my eyes,
guard the gateway of my soul,
and make your counsels easy to me who wills to follow you.

ref: The Eye of the Needle · Matthew 19:23–26
text: Lord Jesus Christ, grant to me, wretched and unworthy,
that for Your sake and for love of You,
and for the glorifying of Your name,
I may be able to leave riches, and delights, and pomps,
and all things that are of the world,
and, among all things, my very self;
and, all else shut out, to cleave to You alone;
and, according to my small measure, to follow You
by whatever imitation of living I can,
that — with You as ruler, with You as guide —
I may deserve to escape the snares and ambushes
of all my enemies, visible and invisible,
and mercifully obtain the rewards
which You have promised to those who leave all things and follow You. Amen.

ref: The Labourers in the Vineyard · Matthew 20:1–16 (with 19:30)
text: Lord Jesus Christ, most high householder,
you led me into your vineyard early in the morning,
when from my youth you mercifully called me to faith and to your service,
and agreed with me to labour for the penny of eternal life;
but I, wretched man, stood idle the whole day of my life,
and by living negligently did not do the work I owed.
Lord, because you are good and merciful,
grant that I may come to my senses at least in the eleventh hour,
and bring forth worthy fruits of penance,
that I may deserve to find with you some wage, however small. Amen.

ref: The Steward Called to Account · Luke 16:1–9
text: Lord Jesus Christ,
make my soul a farm of virtues,
lest your steward, my reason, squander the goods you have entrusted to me;
for after death I know not what meritorious thing I might do.
Forgive me the hundred barrels of oil I owe you through contemplation,
and the hundred measures of wheat I owe you through good works;
and may your poor, who love you and are conformed to you,
become my intercessors, advocates, and patrons before you,
so that when I fail in my own merits, and depart from this life,
I may be received by their merits and prayers into their houses —
into the eternal and beloved dwellings. Amen.

ref: The Rich Man and Lazarus at the Gate · Luke 16:19–21
text: Lord Jesus Christ,
since I am utterly poor in virtues, full of the sores of my sins,
a beggar laid before the gate of your mercy,
longing to be filled with the crumbs of good examples that fall from the table of the rich, your sons,
who are clothed in the purple of the passions and feast sumptuously each day on the dishes of the virtues:
let your dogs, your teachers, come and lick the wounds of my sins,
that, dying to the world, I may be carried by the Angels into the bosom of heavenly contemplation.
Let me hear Moses and the Prophets, turning from evil and doing good,
that, rising from dead works, I may hope to have eternal life.
Amen.

ref: Lazarus, Our Friend, Sleeps · John 11:1–17
text: Lord Jesus, I behold Thy wondrous goodness toward Thine own.
I do not say, Come and heal; I dare not command Thee.
I say only: behold, he whom Thou lovest is sick.
It is enough that Thou knowest, for Thou dost not love and abandon.
Thou who art the consoler of the grieving, the healer of the languishing,
if Thou dost scourge me, break me unto salvation,
and let me not remain whole unto damnation.
Call my death a sleep, for the hope of resurrection;
and wake me, when Thou wilt, out of sleep.
Strengthen me in faith, that seeing, I may more firmly believe in Thee.

ref: The Plot Against the Lord · John 11:47–52
text: Lord Jesus Christ, Angel of great counsel,
who permitted the council of the malicious to be formed against you,
and willed not to resist them by power, but rather to yield by patience:
do not abandon me in erring judgment,
nor in the harmful counsel of my own will or of men,
nor in the power or temptation of demons;
but you yourself, gently, within and without, order and rule me,
and direct me into the way of eternal salvation.
Grant me, Lord, that whatever adversities of devil or of men contrive against me
may be brought to nothing,
so that, unharmed and snatched from every peril,
I may joyfully render to you my thanksgiving. Amen.

ref: The Ten Lepers · Luke 17:11–19
text: Lord Jesus Christ, trusting in your tenderness,
I, like a leper, sprinkled over with the stains of many sins, flee to you, the merciful physician;
I, unclean, befouled with the spots of my vices, run to the fountain of mercies,
and as a suppliant I beg you: deign to heal my sickness, to wash away my foulness, and to guide me to salvation.
Grant that I may always hold your benefits in my heart, and give you thanks for all things;
but because I am a mortal man, ash and dust,
and cannot render even one thanks for a thousand,
let the Virgin Mary and all the citizens of heaven render you thanks for me,
and every creature that can be matter for the divine praise. Amen.

ref: The Closed Door · Luke 9:51-56
text: Lord Jesus Christ,
who didst not avenge Thyself on the Samaritans who denied Thee welcome,
but, bearing it patiently, didst humbly turn aside to another place:
grant to me, unworthy, O sweet guest of the soul,
that, all vices cast out, I may make ready a welcome pleasing to Thee in my heart;
and if ever, through my own deserts, Thou art not received there,
be not angry with me, nor turn away in wrath from Thy servant, nor take vengeance upon me;
but Thou, who camest not to destroy souls but to save them,
reconcile me to Thyself through Thy mercy,
and grant that I may open my heart to Thee by worthy preparation,
and that it may please Thee to enter in to me Thy servant, and mercifully to bless me. Amen.

ref: The Cup He Came to Drink · Matthew 20:17–19; Mark 10:32–34; Luke 18:31–33
text: Lord Jesus Christ,
who for our salvation willed to drink the cup of the Passion,
and to be drenched in the baptism of Your blood,
and came not to rule and be ministered to in worldly fashion,
but humbly to serve and to minister,
and to give Your life for our redemption:
grant to me, a wretched one, according to my small measure,
to become a partaker of Your cup and Your baptism, with patience and joy of spirit,
and to imitate You, my Master and Lord, in the works of humility,
and not only to spend my ministry on my neighbors in works of mercy,
but even to lay down my life for them, if it be necessary,
and at last to come happily to the heavenly kingdom prepared for Your elect.
Amen.

ref: The Blind Man at Jericho · Luke 18:35–43
text: Jesus, Son of David, have mercy on me.
Lord, that I may see.
You who do not disdain the poor and the lowly, stand still for me.
Lay bare my misery before you, and grant me the light you would give out of your own goodness.
Let me not only receive the light, but follow you in the light, glorifying God,
lest I walk in darkness. Amen.

ref: The Man in the Sycamore · Luke 19:1–6
text: Lord Jesus Christ,
who looked with mercy on Zacchaeus climbing into the sycamore,
and calling him to yourself and turning aside into his house, healed him of every vice:
grant to me, little and wretched as I am,
that I may be able to climb the tree of penance,
where you may deign to look on me with the eyes of mercy,
and to call me to your grace;
granting me to prepare the house of my conscience worthily to receive you,
and through you to be wholly healed, within and without.
And you who came from heaven to seek and to save what had perished on earth,
seek me, who through my fault had perished, O good Shepherd,
and make me whole, O loving Saviour. Amen.

ref: The Two Blind Men Beyond Jericho · Matthew 20:29–34; Mark 10:46–52
text: Light of light, and fountain of brightness, Lord Jesus,
who, moved with mercy at the love of the two blind men crying out to Thee,
didst deign to touch their eyes and so enlighten them:
touch, I beseech Thee, my spiritual eyes,
and with the rays of Thy light illumine my conscience —
to contemplate Thee, to know myself, to instruct and help my neighbor.
Illumine my heart with the light of Thy divine grace,
that I may have Thee as my guide in all my ways, and follow Thee by imitating Thee;
and that I may, above all things, fear and love Thee who art above all,
and in all things do Thy will. Amen.

ref: The House of Obedience · Matthew 26:6–7; Mark 14:3; John 12:1–3
text: Lord Jesus Christ,
who, about to suffer for the whole world, willed to come and abide in Bethany, that is, in the house of obedience,
and there to sup, and there to let your head and your feet be anointed:
grant me, persevering in true obedience, to be worthy to make for you this spiritual refreshment;
for you are delighted in our obedience, and in a manner are fed by it.
Make me also there to anoint your head and your feet:
the head, confessing with worthy reverence and praise the power of your divine virtue;
the feet, worthily receiving the mysteries of your assumed humanity;
or the head, by venerating you, O Christ, and the feet, by serving your faithful ones,
that so acting I may obtain your grace. Amen.

ref: The Lord Seated upon the Colt · Matthew 21:1–3; Mark 11:1–3; Luke 19:29–31; John 12
text: Lord Jesus Christ, who came of Your own will to the Passion,
come also to me, a wretched one, by grace into my heart;
and who then restored me by the cross, restore me, fallen again, by that same blessed Passion.
Grant me, Lord, that in all things I may keep humility and meekness,
and may subject to You wholly my flesh and my spirit,
and that I may become as a beast of burden before You;
that, seated in me, and ruling me as Your beast,
You may lead me now to the taste of inward peace, and the desire of the peace above,
and at last bring me into the vision of that same peace. Amen.

ref: The King Comes Riding · Matthew 21; Mark 11; Luke 19; John 12
text: Do not fear; acknowledge him who is praised by you,
and do not tremble when he suffers,
because that blood is poured out
by which your offense may be blotted away,
and your life redeemed.
O King, Christ the Redeemer,
to you be glory, and praise, and honor.
Save, I beseech you: O save me.
