ref: The Lord's Weeping over Jerusalem · Luke 19:41–44
text: Lord Jesus Christ,
who by word and by example
taught us to weep rather than to laugh,
I beseech you,
by your most blessed tears
and by all your acts of mercy,
teach me always to see and to know
my sins and the dangers that press upon me;
lest my enemies — the suggestions of demons,
the beauties of temporal things, and the pleasures of the flesh —
surround me, hem me in, and dash me to the ground;
lest they destroy my children — that is, my senses,
my thoughts and my acts, and their right order;
that thereby I may praise you,
and confess your name, O Most High. Amen.

ref: The Cleansing of the Temple · Matthew 21:12–13
text: Lord Jesus, Lord of the temple,
who came straight to Your Father's house and drove out all that defiled it,
made for Yourself a scourge of little cords and overturned the tables of greed,
come into the temple of my heart;
drive out from me all trafficking, all usury, all that is not consecrated to You,
that nothing be carried through me for the sake of gain,
but that I may be a vessel dedicated to God,
and my soul made again a house of prayer.
Amen.

ref: The Widow's Two Coins · Mark 12:41–44; Luke 18:9–14
text: O God, be merciful to me, a sinner.
I am not as the proud, who trusting in their own justice
pray thus within themselves, as though not before You:
God, I give You thanks that I am not as the rest of men.
No — all that I live is not of my own merit, but of Your gift.
O God, be merciful to me, a sinner.

ref: The Withered Fig Tree · Matthew 21:18–19; John 12:20–23
text: Lord Jesus Christ, grant me to have not only leaves — that is, words and the esteem of justice —
but the fruit of works, that I may never incur Your curse.
Grant that, as Your true servant, I may follow You according to my small measure,
that where You are, there I too may deserve to be.
Draw me after You, good Jesus;
let not the sweetness of the world draw me, but let the sweetness of Your love draw me.
Let my intention be ever with You in heaven, and let Your protection be ever with me on earth.
Let me cling to You inseparably, serve You perseveringly,
seek You faithfully, find You happily, possess You eternally,
my King and my God. Amen.

ref: The Vineyard Let Out to Tenants · Matthew 21:33–43
text: The vineyard has been let out to us, O Lord,
and it is let out on this condition:
that we render the fruit to the Lord in his seasons,
and that we know, each one of us,
what we owe of the fruit of our vineyard.
Make us husbandmen who till well and neglect not,
who increase and diminish not,
who keep and destroy not;
and the fifth grape, the glory, we give not to ourselves, but to you.

ref: The Vineyard and the Blood-Stained Tenants · Matthew 21:33–43
text: A vineyard has been let out to us, O Lord,
and let out on this condition:
that we render the fruit to you in its seasons.
Let us know, each one of us, that we must give back what is yours —
not the glory to ourselves, but to you the glory of God.
Make us happy tenants who till well and do not neglect,
who increase and do not diminish,
who keep and do not destroy;
that we be not cast into the outer darkness,
but render the fruit to you in its seasons.

ref: The Wedding Garment · Matthew 22:1–14
text: Lord Jesus Christ, King of heavenly glory,

who through Thy messengers, that is, through the sacred Scripture, through infirmities, through the peril of living, through the turning of fortune, and through Thy free will, hast called us to Thy marriage:
give us the wedding garment of charity,
that we may never fall silent from the confession of sin and from the praise of God,
that we may never be separated from the resurrection of spiritual joy, that is, from peace and from security;
and give us the garment of piety and of good working,
lest, with hands and feet bound, we be cast into the outer darkness.
Amen.

ref: The Image on the Coin · Matthew 22:15–22; Luke 20:20–26
text: Lord Jesus Christ,
teach me to understand and to beware the cunning of seducers;
and to keep always the truth of life, of doctrine, and of justice.
Grant that I may be marked with your image, not the enemy's,
that by leaving behind the things of the world, and clinging to you alone,
I may render back to you the things I have received from your grace,
serving you faithfully in them.
Grant me also to flee the carnal and worldly way of life, which is barren,
and to follow the spiritual and heavenly one;
that, made new by the glory of the resurrection,
I may deserve to enjoy immortality with the Angels of God in heaven,
and the everlasting vision of you. Amen.

ref: The Greatest Commandment · Matthew 22:34–40
text: Lord, he loves thee less who loves anything together with thee, which he loves not for thy sake.
Thou alone, without any rival, dost will to possess my heart.
Grant me to love thee with my whole heart, that it be inclined to no thing more than to thee.
With my whole soul, holding a most certain mind in thy truth, and standing firm in faith.
With my whole mind, that all my senses may be empty for thee alone.
Let whatever else comes into my soul to be loved be caught up into the one flood of love that runs wholly toward thee.
For a man is then at his best, when with his whole life he presses on toward thee, the unchangeable Good.
Glorifying thee, O Lord, as much as ever I can, thou wilt still surpass it; for thou art greater than all praise.
And so let me love my neighbor as myself, willing him, as I will for myself, to be in grace here and in glory hereafter. Amen.

ref: In the Seat of Moses · Matthew 23:1–4
text: Lord Jesus Christ, in your mercy, take from me the failure of good works,
that I may deserve not only to think and to speak good things, but also to do them, and to fulfill them in deeds.
Remove from me austerity and cruelty,
lest by commanding great things and not doing the lesser, I be seen to relieve myself and to burden others.
Shut out from me vainglory and ostentation,
lest I do my works to be seen by men, and seek human glory in them.
Drive far from me the craving for ambition and praise,
that I may not love the first places, nor the greetings, nor the name of master;
so that, humbling myself thus in this age, I may deserve to be exalted by you in the age to come. Amen.

ref: The Eight Woes · Matthew 23:13–15
text: Lord Jesus Christ,
who give blessings to those who obey your Law and your precepts,
and maledictions to the disobedient:
grant me of your bounty always to obey those precepts of yours,
and in all things to shun the errors of the hypocrites.
Grant me to rest in your warnings and your good pleasure without weariness,
and to amend myself without negligence;
that at last, at the coming of your brightness unto judgement,
I may look with gladness upon you, the Blessed One,
coming in open manifestation unto our full redemption,
the perfect freedom of body and soul,
and with your elect may bless you without end. Amen.

ref: The Signs Before the End · Matthew 24:1–8; Mark 13; Luke 21
text: Lord Jesus Christ, Judge of all who art to come,
grant me constancy in the truth of the faith,
that I be not seduced by feigned and false men,
nor sink down beneath my tribulations;
but in the confession of Thy name, and in the assertion of the truth,
may I be able to stand firm with strength,
and for this to bear gladly the hatred of men and all adversity.
Grant me the virtue of patience,
that, though iniquity abound, the ardour of charity grow not cold in me;
but that, even to the end of this present life,
I may persevere in the truth of the faith and in the love of goodness,
so that, by Thy mercy, I may be saved,
O Lord God of my salvation. Amen.

ref: The Abomination of Desolation · Matthew 24; Mark 13; Luke 17
text: Lord Jesus Christ, in your mercy,
grant me, when I have seen the abominations of the world and the raging tribulations,
to flee to the mountains and the heights of the virtues;
and, dwelling among heavenly things,
not to come down from spiritual loftiness to carnal desires and the acts of my former worldly life,
nor to bend myself back to them.
Stretching forward to what lies ahead, and laboring for the fruit of good works,
let me not look back to the things behind, to the temporal things of which I have stripped myself.
Grant me also, believing no seducers,
to seek the kingdom of grace, which can be within us,
and so to come to the kingdom of glory. Amen.

ref: Remedies Against the Last Deceptions · Matthew 24:24
text: Lord Jesus Christ, meek and humble of heart,
ground my heart in true humility,
and shut out from me every motion of presumption and pride,
that I may never desire visions or revelations,
nor be led astray by any spiritual temptations
that could in any way draw me into error.
Make me, most loving Lord, to rise each day to the works of the virtues,
and ever to advance in good,
and continually to tend and sigh toward a higher life;
that thus, by watching and acting carefully,
I may be able to prepare myself for Your uncertain coming. Amen.

ref: Watching and Praying for the Coming · Luke 18:1–8; 21:36
text: Lord Jesus Christ,
grant me to imitate the signs in the sun, moon, and stars,
that is, the examples of good works
in You, the Sun of justice,
in the Virgin Mother, and in all Your saints;
that in the earth of my carnality
the distress of the nations may arise,
the confusion of the sea's sound and the roaring of contrition,
while in me the human desires wither away,
for fear of hell and longing for the glory
that shall come upon the whole world,
when You shall render to each according to his works;
that the powers of the soul be moved from virtue to virtue,
so that I may be found ready at Your coming.
Amen.

ref: The Sign of the Cross in Heaven · Matthew 24:30; Luke 21:27
text: Lord Jesus Christ, who are to come to judge with power and great majesty,
while the Angels bear before You the sign of the glorious Cross as a triumphal banner,
seal my soul with the imprint of this most holy Cross;
purify me by its power;
and through it claim me wholly for Yourself,
so that the adversary may find nothing in me;
that when You come to judgment, I may be found marked with this venerable sign,
and, conformed to You crucified in suffering,
may merit to become a sharer also in You risen in glory. Amen.

ref: Lift Up Your Heads · Luke 21:28–33 (with Matthew 24 and Mark 13)
text: Lord Jesus Christ, come to me in the clouds of penitence,
and dissolve my heart into a rain of tears.
Cast out the demons that hold power over my vices,
and reign in me by your majesty.
Make me, O our Redemption, to look upon you with inward eyes,
and to lift up the head of my mind.
Grant me to see the fig tree —
to feel, that is, the sweetness of your love —
that the trees of all the virtues may bring forth in me the fruit of good works,
and that the summer of the kingdom of heaven may be near to me.
Grant me to beware of every vice,
and to watch in continual prayer,
that I may merit to flee the evils to come,
and in the judgment to stand secure before you.
Amen.

ref: The Day That Comes Without Warning · Matthew 24:37–39; Luke 17:26–30
text: Lord Jesus Christ,
grant me to go before the uncertainty of the day of your coming
by watching and by working well,
lest, given over to pleasures and to vanities,
I be found suddenly unprepared on that day.
Grant me then to be counted, by your grace, among your elect,
and with them to be mercifully taken up to joys eternal;
and so, with those same elect and your saints —
whose youth shall be renewed like the eagle's through the resurrection —
may I, by your mercy, where you are gathered in your glorified body,
be made worthy to be filled with the sight of you,
and, rejoicing, to praise you without ceasing, without end.
Amen.

ref: The Watchful Householder · Matthew 24:43–44; Mark 13:35–37
text: Lord Jesus Christ,
grant me unceasingly to ponder the day of my passing and the day of judgment,
and to foresee my last things,
that I may watch carefully for things spiritual and eternal,
guarding myself from sins, and pressing on in good works;
that I may not neglect myself,
but at every hour prepare and dispose myself for my death.
Grant, O Lord, that I may provide each day for my own ending,
and that my whole life may be such in its living
that I may deserve to be free in the end,
and to receive You, coming in death or in judgment, rejoicing,
and to rejoice with You without end. Amen.

ref: Loins Girded, Lamps Burning · Luke 12:35–37
text: Lord Jesus Christ,
who commanded us both to bind fast our loins and to hold our lamps burning,
do You Yourself gird our loins,
that no iniquity may rule over us, and that we may turn aside to no sin.
Do You Yourself make our lamps burn, and guard them burning,
that our charity may not grow cold,
but that our every thought, word, and work
may always be directed to the doing of Your justice.
Make me, Lord, to await Your coming watchfully and earnestly,
that, receiving You with joy, I may be able — by Your mercy —
to come to the joys eternal. Amen.

ref: The Ten Virgins · Matthew 25:1–7
text: Lord Jesus Christ, good Bridegroom,
grant me, with the wise virgins, to do good works with right intention,
and watchfully to persevere in them,
that, found ready, I may enter with gladness into Your wedding feast.
I ask, O Lord, that You make known to me my end before it comes,
and do not let me be finished before You have forgiven my sins,
that in the hour of my passing the divine vengeance may not fall upon me,
and the power of darkness may not meet me, nor harm me;
but let the day of examination give me back such as the font of regeneration once made me. Amen.

ref: The Talents Entrusted · Matthew 25:14–30 (with Mark 13, Luke 19)
text: Lord Jesus Christ,
who in mercy have handed over to us your gifts,
and warn us, watchful and diligent,
to bring forth fruit from them:
grant me, your unworthy servant,
to trade watchfully with the gifts I have received from you,
and to multiply them by the fruit of good works;
and so to use them well —
for your glory, my neighbor's building up,
and my own perfection —
that I may deserve to be rewarded with your good and faithful servants,
and, at your bidding, to enter the joy of eternal happiness,
and to receive what you have prepared for those who love you,
O good Jesus. Amen.

ref: The Winnowing of the Threshing-Floor · Matthew 25:31–34
text: Lord Jesus Christ, judge of the living and the dead,
in the last judgement deign to set me at thy right hand,
and then make me to hear that most sweet voice of thine,
which thou hast promised to thine elect, saying:
Come, ye blessed of my Father, receive the kingdom;
and grant that I may not fear that evil hearing thou hast threatened to the reprobate,
saying: Depart, ye cursed, into everlasting fire.
O only-begotten Son of God, have mercy on us,
that we may never undergo that incurable pain —
to fall away from the glory of the heavenly kingdom, and to be burned in hell.
My God, my mercy. Amen.

ref: Behold and Do · Matthew 26 (with Exodus 25:40; 1 Peter 2:21; 1 Corinthians 2:2)
text: Lord Jesus Christ,
who on the third day before the time of your Passion and death
foretold to your disciples your Pasch and your passing out of this world,
grant me, through all the time of my life,
to live in the unleavened bread of sincerity and truth;
and let me not pass out of this world
before, through a three-day penance —
that is, through contrition of heart, confession of mouth, and satisfaction of deed —
you blot out all my sins,
granting me the communion of your most sacred Body and Blood,
the anointing of holy oil, and in all things a perfect consummation;
and at the last, happily consummated,
make me pass over to you, my most sweet Lord, blessed above all things. Amen.

ref: The Price of a Friend · Matthew 26:14–16; Luke 22:1–6
text: O most merciful Jesus,
who bore the labors of preaching, the weariness of journeying,
the insults and reproaches, the injuries and the anguish, for our salvation,
and at last willed to be sold by one of your own disciples,
and to be priced by the Jews at thirty pieces of silver,
and so to be sold at a worthless price:
grant me to imitate the example of such patience and humility,
and by your grace and your counsel to overcome with patience
the reproaches, injuries, and all things laid upon me,
and never to barter you wickedly away for any passing thing,
and to bear my own contempt and anguish, with even mind, for the glory of your name.
Amen.

ref: The Supper Prepared · Luke 22:7–13 (with Matthew 26 and Mark 14)
text: Lord Jesus Christ,
who at the hour of vespers kept the last supper with your disciples
in the great upper room, furnished,
and fed them with your most sacred Body and Blood:
make my breast an upper room, great and furnished;
enlarge hope and charity in my heart;
magnify in me long-suffering, hope and charity,
patience and humility.
Grant that my heart, contrite and humbled,
may receive you in its small measure —
you whom heaven and earth cannot contain;
that through your indwelling grace
I may think and accomplish all things pleasing to you,
and turn away from all things hateful and contrary;
and so, persevering to the end,
I may then attain a worthy receiving
of your most holy Body and Blood.

ref: The Washing of the Feet · John 13:1–8
text: O Jesus, gentle example of true humility,
who didst wash the feet of thy disciples,
I ask and beseech thee, Lord, purge my affections,
that, with both feet — that is, with the twofold love —
cleansed and set aflame,
I may come secure to thee, my purifier.
Purify me unto the end of my days,
and cleanse me from every stain of sin,
that, my negligences and sins forgiven,
the enemies who watch my heel
may fall back confounded on the day of my death.
Direct, O Lord, my feet into the way of peace,
that, delivered from the hands of all my enemies,
I may bless thee with all thine elect,
unto the ages of ages. Amen.

ref: The Warning at the Table · Matthew 26:21–25; John 13:21–26
text: Lord Jesus Christ, good Shepherd,
who willed in many ways to call Your disciple back from his errors to repentance,
call me back from all my wanderings and from all my errors.
Give me, my Savior, the power and the strength to conquer the devil,
who keeps watch continually and wars against me,
lest by his most wicked deceit he drag me to the pit of darkness;
but, upheld by Your help, may I come to the brightness of glory,
where the just, made radiant in the likeness of the angelic company,
shall shine as the sun, in the kingdom of their Father. Amen.

ref: The Bread He Became · Matthew 26:26–28; Mark 14:22–24; Luke 22:19–20
text: Lord Jesus, who didst hand over and leave to us Thy very self,
Thy most holy Body and Blood, truly for our food and drink:
grant me, in receiving and before receiving this sacrament,
devotion and prayer, and the lifting up of my mind to Thee.
Let me receive Thee with mind and body,
and eat Thee with faith and with mouth,
and taste and see in devotion that Thou art sweet.
Change me not into Thyself as I change earthly food,
but let me be changed into Thee.
And what I cannot do sacramentally, let me do by faith,
for Thou hast said: Believe, and thou hast eaten.

ref: Let Not Your Heart Be Troubled · John 13:33; 14:1–3, 18, 27; 16:7
text: O Lord, prepare what you are preparing:
us, that is, for yourself, and yourself for us,
that there may be a multitude of mansions for the diversity of merits:
one more, another less, we shall be sharers of you.
Do not leave us orphans;
go and come to us, sending the Holy Spirit,
that where you are, we also may be.

ref: As If You Were Present · Galatians 6:14; Psalm 126:5; Romans 8:17
text: Most sweet Lord Jesus,
pour into me, a sinner, I beseech You, the fullness of Your love;
that I may desire nothing earthly or carnal,
but love You alone above all things,
so that my soul may utterly refuse to be consoled,
save in You, my most sweet God.
Write with Your finger upon the tablets of my heart
the memory of all that You bore for me,
that I may have it ever before my eyes;
and grant that it may grow sweet to me not only to ponder these things,
but also, if need be, to bear them in my small measure;
and not only to serve You with all my strength,
but even, for Your sake, to suffer reproach,
or to be condemned to the basest death.
Amen.

ref: The Agony in the Garden · Luke 22:39–44 (with Matthew 26 and Mark 14)
text: Lord Jesus Christ,
who for me, a wretch, willed to be sorrowful and heavy,
grant me to bear all my sorrows unceasingly toward you;
and may you deign to bear them with me,
joined to your own sorrow.
O Jesus, who being set in prayer willed to be strengthened by an angel,
grant me, by the power of your prayer,
that your angel may always stand by me as I pray, strengthening me.

ref: Matins: The First Interrogation · John 18:19–23 (with Matthew 26, Mark 14, Luke 22)
text: O Lord Jesus, how do You sit so despised and desolate?
Where are Your disciples and Your friends?
O my one good! O my one and only joy!
What shall I do, seeing You like this?
Surely, Lord, I will sit on the ground with You,
and keep You company,
for I see no one here who loves You,
but rather Your enemies, raging and mad, who hate You.
Lord Jesus Christ, who at the morning hour, before Annas,
received a blow from a servant,
and let Yourself be led, bound, to Caiaphas,
and through the whole night bore many insults and afflictions:
grant me, a sinner, I beseech You,
that I may rejoice to suffer the like for the glory of Your name,
and may deserve to enjoy those eternal joys.
With those same eyes of Your tender mercy,
with which You then looked upon Peter,
look often upon me, wretched as I am,
who so many times denied You
by evil thoughts, words, deeds, and neglects,
that I may come to know my faults and repent of them,
and deserve to find pardon and grace with You. Amen.

ref: The Council of the Malignant · Luke 22:66–71; Matthew 27:1–4
text: Lord Jesus Christ,
who at the first hour of the day suffered yourself to be led, bound, into the council of the Jews,
and after many injuries and revilings to be led from Caiaphas to Pilate,
where you were judged by the Jews a man guilty of death and a malefactor,
and were falsely accused of many things;
and then to be sent from Pilate to Herod, and there accused and reckoned a fool,
and despised, and mocked in a white garment,
and at last, so mocked and clothed in white, sent back to Pilate:
grant me, I beseech you, to bear injuries and revilings patiently and gladly for the glory of your name,
and to appear before your face with joy,
and ever to gaze upon your desirable countenance. Amen.

ref: Terce: The Silent Lamb · Matthew 27:11–31; Mark 15:1–20; Luke 23:1–25; John 19:1–16
text: Lord Jesus Christ, who at the third hour of the day willed to be scourged for us,
free me from the scourges of that eternal wrath which I have deserved;
by the piercings of your most sacred head, pierce my mind,
that I may have strength to shun the delights that harm me,
and grant me, after penance, to attain to the crown.
You who, mocked in so many ways, were at last sentenced and condemned to death,
make me escape the devil's mockery and eternal death.
You bore your cross, O Lord: grant me to take up my cross and follow you —
my own cross, the one owed to my sins,
and my own cross willingly and devoutly borne,
that through it I may come to glory. Amen.

ref: The Place of the Skull · Matthew 27:33–34; Mark 15:22–23; John 19:17; Psalm 68:22 (Vulg.)
text: Lord Jesus, they gave you gall for your food,
and in your thirst they gave you vinegar to drink;
they sought to embitter the inmost heart of you, most sweet Lord.
You suffered outside the gate — let me go out to you.
Outside the camp, outside the city of this world,
bearing your reproach and your pain.
Let me leave the world in love, that I may not love it;
leave it in deed, forsaking it wholly in mind and body;
that, going forward, I may pant to become one spirit with you.
For a man is near to you, my God, in the measure that he is a stranger to the love of the world.

ref: Into Your Hands · Luke 23:46; Matthew 27:50; Mark 15:37; John 19:30
text: Lord Jesus Christ,
who at the ninth hour of the day, hanging upon the gibbet,
and crying with a loud voice, commended Your spirit into the hands of the Father,
and, with bowed head, sent forth that same spirit,
and, now dead, received in Your side the wound from the soldier's lance:
deign, I beseech You, now and always, to hold my spirit commended to You,
and to pierce my heart through with the sword of charity,
and to imprint upon it the wounds of Your body,
and by these to drive far from it all unlawful thoughts,
and at the last, at the end of my life,
to place my spirit, commended into Your hands, among the blessed spirits. Amen.

ref: The Lowering of the Body · Matthew 27; Mark 15; Luke 23; John 19
text: Lord Jesus Christ,
who at the evening hour willed to be taken down from the cross by Joseph,
and to be received between his arms;
who wast also bewailed by thy most sorrowful Mother and thy other friends:
make me, I beseech thee, a Joseph, growing daily in virtues;
and grant that, mourning over the sins for which thou wast crucified
and art again, as it were, bound upon the cross,
I may be worthy to take thee down from that cross;
and to receive thee, like a little bundle of myrrh, between the arms of my love,
where thou, my Beloved, mayest deign forever to abide,
and keep me from the snares of the devil,
that I may never, here or hereafter, be parted from thee.
Amen.

ref: The Anointing and the Tomb · John 19:38–42; Matthew 27:59–60; Mark 15:46; Luke 23:53
text: Lord Jesus Christ,
who at the hour of Compline willed to be anointed and prepared with spices,
to be wrapped and bound in the shroud and the other linens,
and, while Your Mother and Your other friends lamented,
to be carried to burial and laid in the tomb:
grant me, I pray,
that I may anoint You with spices, through fervent devotion and a good life;
that I may wrap You in shroud and linens, through pure affection and cleanness of conscience;
that I may mourn You with the tears of penitence and compassion,
and carry You in the arms of charitable and humble labor;
and that I may bury You in my heart, through unceasing remembrance and memory;
so that I too, buried together with You,
may deserve to come with You to the glory of the resurrection. Amen.

ref: The Praise of the Cross · Matthew 26:53; cf. Luke 23:1–46; Isaiah 53:5
text: Lord Jesus Christ,
who for the redemption of the world willed to bear patiently miseries and anguish,
reproaches and insults, slanders and injuries, pains and afflictions, the Passion and death:
through all these things which you endured for our sins,
free me from all sins and vices,
from all the perils of this world, from infernal punishments,
from sudden and from eternal death.
And grant to me, I beseech you,
not to flee or to forget all that you endured for me,
but to hold it always before my eyes, and to embrace it ardently;
so that, sharing in your labor and your sorrow,
you may will me also to be a partaker of your rest and your consolation.
Amen.

ref: The One Lamp Still Burning · Mark 16:1; cf. Matthew 12:40
text: O good Jesus,
the ineffable mercy of Your tenderness was not yet satisfied,
until, breaking through the bars of hell also,
You should redeem Your captives.
That blessed and most holy soul of Yours descended to the dead,
leading the bound out of darkness and the shadow of death.
Descend now also, merciful Jesus,
through this ineffable tenderness of Yours,
with Your grace and mercy
upon the souls of Your servants,
of my parents and kindred,
that You may snatch them from the pains they deserve for their sins,
and lead them to joys eternal. Amen.

ref: The New Day After the Sabbath · Matthew 28:1-2
text: Lord Jesus Christ, sole sweetness,
who, the chains of death being broken, glorified your body,
and rose in glory so unspeakable:
I beg and beseech you, by your flowering Resurrection,
grant that I, rising from vices and from the death of the soul,
may ever flower in virtues, and walk in newness of life;
that I may seek and savor the things that are above,
not the things that are upon the earth.
By the power of your brightness,
cleanse my soul from the darkness of sins;
and by that same power, on the day of the universal resurrection,
let my flesh rise again to glory,
that with you I may rejoice eternally in both. Amen.

ref: The First to See Him Risen · Cf. John 21:25; Mark 16:9 (Ludolf's pious tradition of the Resurrection)
text: O Mary, Mother of God and gracious Virgin,
true consoler of all the desolate who cry out to you:
by that great joy with which you were consoled,
when you knew the Lord Jesus had risen on the third day from the dead, beyond all suffering,
be the consoler of my soul;
and before that same your Son, the only-begotten of God,
on the last day, when I shall rise again with soul and body,
and must render an account of each of my deeds,
deign to help me,
that through you, holy Mother and Virgin,
I may escape the sentence of everlasting damnation,
and with all God's chosen come happily to joys eternal. Amen.

ref: At the Tomb Before Dawn · Matthew 28; Mark 16; Luke 24; John 20 (esp. Matt 28:1; Mark 16:2; Luke 24:1; John 20:1)
text: Let us therefore imitate these same holy and most devout women,
that as they with their spices sought the Lord
with their whole heart and desire in the sepulchre,
which is the proper place of the dead,
so we too, with our own spices —
the working of good works,
the fragrance of the virtues,
the sweetness of prayers —
may set out with the whole affection of the mind toward God,
and through holy desires seek Him,
not in the grave, but in heaven.
Amen.

ref: All Hail · Matthew 28:9–10; John 20:18
text: O Jesus, our redemption, our love and our longing,
who to the devout women seeking You, desiring You,
and returning from Your tomb,
rising from the dead in mercy willed to come and appear —
I seek You, whom my soul loves, O sweetest Jesus;
I seek, and I do not find:
come, then, to my soul that seeks You,
O only Son of God the Father.
And if You delay Your desirable presence now,
do not take it from me in the time to come;
that in the last resurrection of the faithful
You may show Yourself to me, gentle and kind, with them,
and that of Your presence I may forever rejoice with Your elect. Amen.

ref: The Lie of the Guards · Matthew 28:11–15
text: Lord Jesus Christ,
to blacken the fame of Your Resurrection the Jews gave a large sum of money to the soldiers, and corrupted them to pervert the truth.
Grant me that I be not corrupted by money, nor corrupt others,
nor act unjustly for things temporal and passing,
but always remain in the truth, and be set free through it.
Grant me also that, as after Your Resurrection there rose up witnesses to You,
so I too may be worthy to testify of You in word and work,
and in the general resurrection to have my portion with You and with Your elect,
and with them, before You in Your kingdom, to rejoice without end, in Your mercy. Amen.

ref: He Sought, and Was Found · Luke 24:34; cf. 1 Corinthians 15:5–7
text: Lord Jesus Christ, grant that I may be Peter by obeying,
and James by trampling down my vices,
and Joseph, going from virtue to virtue by increase,
and in this persevering with long-suffering;
that You, who deigned to appear to them in their distress,
may deign also often to visit me, mercifully, by Your grace.
Grant me too, that as the holy Fathers had joy in Your presence
when they were counted worthy to see You glorified after Your Resurrection,
so I also may deserve to rejoice forever in Your face with them,
in that unspeakable joy with which they now rejoice with You in heaven. Amen.

ref: The Stranger on the Road · Luke 24:13–27
text: Lord Jesus, faithful friend, faithful companion, kind Lord,
you could not bear to see your own so grieved and so astray.
When I am weighed down by perplexity and weariness,
and speak of you devoutly upon the road,
be at once present to me.
Strengthen my heart and enlighten it,
and set it aflame with love of you.
Journey with me, and do not only journey,
but teach me, opening the Scriptures of yourself,
that my eyes, now held by doubt, may know you.
For you do not forget those who are mindful of you;
and those who remember your Passion you accompany the more closely.
Be in the midst of us, as you promised,
where two or three are gathered in your name. Amen.

ref: Peace Be With You · Luke 24:33–36; John 20:19–20
text: Lord Jesus, you who came in through the closed doors,
stand now in the midst of us.
You said to your own: Peace be with you —
not the peace of time, but the peace of the heart and the peace of eternity.
Peace I leave with you, my own peace I give to you.
It is I; do not be afraid.
Where two or three are gathered in your name, there you are in the midst of them.
Stand, then, in the midst of me, as the sun among the stars,
that you may give me light, and adorn me, and make me strong, and give me life.

ref: The Wound and the Word · John 20:24–29
text: My Lord and my God.
Thou art my Lord, who redeemed me with Thy blood and made me Thine own;
Thou art my God, who created me and formed me from the slime of the earth.
Now I doubt nothing; I am certain.
I confirm Thy Resurrection; I proclaim Thy immortality.
Thou art my Lord and my Master; I am Thy servant, and the son of Thy handmaid.
Let me put my hand into that sweetest side, that most healing side —
the side whence we are saved from wrath,
whence we are healed of our guilt,
whence we are reborn into grace,
whence we are lifted up into glory.
This is my faith: thus I feel, and thus I believe.
And though I have not seen Thee in the flesh, make me blessed in believing. Amen.

ref: The Lord on the Shore · John 21:1–7
text: Lord Jesus Christ, grant me, a wretched man, to set before you the food you ask,
by obeying your commandments;
for you are in a manner refreshed by our obedience, and in it you take the greatest delight.
Make me also, unworthy, a sharer of your banquet,
that I may be refreshed by you, the fish roasted and suffered for us, by suffering for you,
and be filled by you, the true bread, now in your teaching and the sacrament of your Body,
and at last in the joy of the eternal fellowship.
Grant me also, Lord, that with Peter I may be worthy to love you, bearing evils for your sake,
and with John to be loved by you, clinging to you;
and ever to grow in this, and to persevere unto the end. Amen.

ref: On the Mountain in Galilee · Matthew 28:16–20 (with 1 Corinthians 15:6)
text: O Lord Jesus Christ, to whom is given all power in heaven and in earth,
exalted in your humanity, set at the right hand of the Father,
raised above all the angels,
and given the name which is above every name,
that at the name of Jesus every knee should bow,
of things in heaven, on earth, and under the earth:
grant me, a wretched sinner, that ascending the mountain with your disciples,
I may tread underfoot all my earthly desires,
and from the lowest pleasures pass over to the things on high.
And though I doubt, draw near to me as you drew near to them,
and shut out the doubting of my heart;
that adoring you living and immortal,
I may know to what glory you have come,
and be with you all my days,
even to the consummation of the world. Amen.

ref: The Forty Days · Acts 1:3
text: Lord Jesus Christ,
by your glorious Resurrection,
in which, living and immortal, you appeared to your disciples and faithful;
by the most gracious lingering and the sweetest conversations
which you had with them through forty days,
appearing to them with many proofs and speaking of the kingdom of God,
strengthening them, and eating with them,
that they might prove and believe the true Resurrection in you,
and you took all doubt away from their hearts:
I beg you, good and merciful Jesus,
grant me, among the witnesses foreordained by God,
to become a witness of your Resurrection,
not by tongue and word alone,
but by deed, and in truth. Amen.

ref: The Lord Ascends into Heaven · Mark 16:19; Luke 24:50–52; Acts 1:9–11
text: Lord Jesus Christ,
who hast deigned, through Scripture and the teaching of the Gospel,
to call us to the catholic faith,
that believing we may have life everlasting,
now indeed in hope, and in the time to come in reality,
and this in Thy name, and through faith in Thy name,
for there is no other name under heaven given to men,
in which we must be saved:
grant to me, unworthy and least of all,
that I may believe in my heart unto justice,
and confess with my mouth unto salvation,
and show forth in my works the fruit and profit of life,
so that, by Thy mercy,
I may now be kindled in mind to the glory of eternal blessedness,
and at last may deserve to come to it in very truth,
O Jesus, Saviour of the world. Amen.

ref: The Struck Cheek · John 18:13–23 (with Matthew 26, Mark 14, Luke 22)
text: Lord Jesus Christ,
who at the hour of Matins, before Annas,
received a blow from an officer,
and allowed yourself, bound, to be led away to Caiaphas,
and through the whole night bore many insults and hardships:
grant me, I beg, a sinner,
that I may rejoice to suffer the like for the glory of your name,
and may deserve to enjoy those eternal joys.
With those same eyes of your tender mercy,
with which you then looked upon Peter,
look often upon me in my wretchedness,
who have so often denied you
by evil thoughts, words, deeds, and neglect,
that I may come to know my faults and repent of them,
and may deserve to find pardon and grace with you. Amen.

ref: The Fire That Does Not Consume · Acts 2:1–4
text: O Holy Spirit, the Spirit of truth,
come now and descend, fill us and console us,
strengthen us and instruct us,
and bestow upon us the fullness of virtues and of joys.
Purge us by holy fear,
melt our hardness by piety,
make firm our weakness by fortitude,
enlighten our darkness by understanding,
and with the inextinguishable ardor of charity kindle us to all love.
Consume in us whatever is worthy to be consumed,
and when it is consumed, be in us no longer a fire that consumes, but one that enlightens.
Give light to the just, and to this wretched sinner give it also;
carry us out of ourselves, and draw us away from the affections of the mind,
and bear off from us the eternal damnation we have deserved.

ref: Made to Praise · Genesis 1:26; Psalm 32:1; Sirach 15:9; Job 9:20
text: O Father of mercies,
I know, and truly I confess,
that it would better befit a wretched sinner to lie prostrate before You,
and with weeping and groaning to beg pardon for his sins,
than to praise You with a polluted mouth.
Yet, trusting in Your most tender goodness and inborn sweetness,
I long to praise You from my inmost heart,
beseeching the depths of Your mercies
that You despise me not, a foul worm, a dead dog, a rotting corpse,
O Lord my God.
For if the Powers of heaven cannot worthily praise You,
how much less can frail man, rottenness and a worm?
Yet not for this must praise be left off;
for there is no creature that does not praise its Maker, or at least leave Him worthy of praise.
Shall I then not praise You, because I know myself unclean?
Do not even the worms and frogs, bred unclean from corruption, desire to praise You their Creator, as far as they know and are able?
Would that every sweet instrument of music, the harmonies of heaven and the melodies of earth, might rise up from the heart and sound forth praises worthy of You.

ref: The Falling-Asleep of the Virgin · Acts 1:14
text: I beg you — if there is in you any heart of compassion —
consider with what love she was tormented,
with what longing this Virgin burned,
while she turned over in her soul all that she had heard,
all that she had seen, all that she had known.
I think that whatever there is of heart, whatever of mind, whatever of human strength,
if you bring it all to bear, it will not suffice
to grasp how unceasingly she was tormented with the fire of holiest love,
how she was moved by desires, filled with the Holy Spirit,
how she was carried away by the wonder of heavenly secrets.

ref: The Judge Returns · Matthew 25:35–41; Matthew 25:6; Revelation 6:16
text: Most kindly Jesus,
who through the boundless patience of your goodness
wait for my amendment:
grant to me, a wretched man,
that I may judge myself now, while I examine myself,
and may have strength to shun all that displeases you,
and to fulfill all that pleases you;
and so may I become pleasing and acceptable to you,
that in the judgement to come,
set with your elect on the right hand,
and finding you favorable and appeased,
I may receive the kingdom prepared for them,
to enjoy forever your longed-for presence,
for which I sigh,
O gentle Jesus,
my desire, my reward, and my life. Amen.

ref: The Pains of Hell and the Glory of Heaven · 1 Corinthians 2:9; cf. Matthew 7:23
text: But let us by no means suffer this, O only-begotten Son of God,
nor let us undergo that intolerable and dreadful punishment.
Let us not be driven from the honor of that blessed glory,
nor be hateful to you, nor hear from you: I never knew you.
Let us not see that face, full of meekness and tenderness, turned away from us,
nor those eyes of all calm and sweetness refuse to look upon us.
Grant us rather that glory and joy of paradise,
where the joys are so many and so great that no reckoner of this world could number them,
which eye has not seen, nor ear heard, nor has it entered into the heart of man.

ref: The Sealed Book · Canticles 1:12-13 (with Song of Songs 2:16)
text: O Jesus Christ, Son of the living God,
for these things, and for all that we devoutly believe of you
and ought to believe,
we render you thanks, though unworthy;
and through them we humbly beseech you,
that you offer your very self to God the Father
on behalf of all things in heaven, on earth, and beneath the earth:
for the worthy praise of all,
in full thanksgiving for every gift owed by each creature,
and for the true amendment of all the evils
committed from the beginning of the world,
and for the satisfaction of all the good ever left undone.
O my sweetest Jesus, hope of the sighing soul,
the tears of the devout seek you,
the cry of the inmost mind calls out to you.
Wherever I shall be, my Jesus I desire.
How glad, when I have found him! How happy, when I hold him!
Now what I sought, I see; what I longed for, I hold;
I languish for the love of Jesus, and with my whole heart I burn.
Amen.
